EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog formed across the Ohio River counties of southwest Indiana. The visibility was at or below one-quarter mile at times during the late night and early morning hours. The dense fog formed in the vicinity of a warm front that extended from the Ozarks of Missouri to just north of the Ohio River.

Lake effect snow showers developed across northwest Indiana the afternoon of January 19th producing some light snow accumulations. An intense single band dropped into LaPorte County during the early morning hours on January 20th, and the band stalled over west and northwest portions of the country producing snow accumulations in excess of 8 inches.  This created hazardous travel conditions with reports of slideoffs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed on the afternoon of January 19th, but a more intense single band of heavy lake effect snow affected the northern and western portions of LaPorte County beginning during the early overnight hours on January 20th. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour accompanied the heavier lake effect snow band with numerous reports of slideoffs. Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches across southeast portions of the county to 6 to 10 inches across the far north and northwest portions of the county. A storm total of 9.5 inches was reported from 1 mile ENE of Westville, IN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of heavy rainfall events in late December and early January sent the Wabash, White, and portions of the Ohio River above flood stage. The river flooding was minor. Low-lying farmland and fields along both rivers were flooded, as well as some river access roads and public recreational land.

A winter storm moved across northwest Indiana during the morning of January 23rd producing 3 to 5 inches of snow.  As the system snow ended, a band of heavy lake effect snow moved across Lake County and then slowly dissipated as it moved into Porter County.  Storm total snowfall amounts in Lake County included 7.0 inches two miles northwest of Hammond; 6.5 inches in Lake Station and Gary and 5.7 inches in Hobart.

Lake effect snow showers developed across northwest and portions of north central Indiana Thursday afternoon producing some additional light snow accumulations and some hazardous travel. An intense single band dropped into northern St. Joseph County Indiana late Thursday evening into the early morning hours of Friday with snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour. Numerous slide-offs were reported and many area schools were closed on Friday January 7. Total snow accumulations from Thursday afternoon into Friday morning ranged from 3 to 6 inches across northern St. Joseph County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed on the afternoon of Thursday January 6th, but a more intense single band of heavy lake effect snow affected northern St. Joseph County late in the evening on January 6th extending into early morning hours of January 7th. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour accompanied the heavier lake effect snow band with numerous reports of slideoffs. Numerous schools were closed on Friday. Snowfall amounts from Thursday afternoon into early Friday morning ranged from 3 to 6 inches. The greatest snowfall occurred across the northern portions of the county.

Areas of freezing drizzle and light snow created a glaze of ice on roads and sidewalks. There were numerous accidents reported across northern Indiana on January 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of freezing drizzle with temperatures in the upper 20s created icy roads on January 17th. There were numerous reports of rollovers and slide-offs, with a few roads briefly closed in the region due to accidents. A thin glaze of ice also developed on sidewalks, with some minor injuries reported from people slipping on the ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very thin layer of dry air in the lowest levels of the atmosphere led to surface temperatures dropping quickly during the early afternoon as light liquid precipitation began falling on western central Indiana.  This scenario caused a brief period of freezing rain, which led to a thin coating of ice and several accidents, two of which proved fatal. A few hours of freezing rain and a thin coating of ice continued across the remainder of central and east central Indiana in the early evening with minor impacts before temperatures rose above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vigo County Sheriff relayed through media that icy and wet roads contributed to a head-on collision of 2 automobiles in Terre Haute on Clinton Street just north of Park Avenue.  1 adult died from the collision.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Indiana State Police relayed through media that icy conditions on Interstate 70 near the westbound 24 mile marker caused a vehicle to lose control and strike a tree.  3 adults died from the collision while a minor sustained non-life-threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow and intense lake effect snow band developed during the late afternoon and evening hours of October 17th. Wet, heavy snow accumulations of 1 to 3 inches led to downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lake effect snow band clipped Whitley County with 1 to 3 inches of heavy wet snow, heaviest across northern portions of the county. This combined with leaves on tress led to several reports of downed trees and power outages across the county. Roads also became snow covered for a time with a few accidents reported across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread winds from the south-southwest to west-southwest, sustained at 20-25 MPH with gusts to 30-40 MPH, rebounded temperatures by about 35-degrees Fahrenheit from an unseasonably cool morning to a seasonably mild afternoon...while promoting relative humidity falling to around 15 percent by the late afternoon. The combination of these factors lead to a large grass fire in Shelby County.||A few smaller fires were observed in Sullivan County.  Other fires likely occurred across central Indiana with relative humidity values of 10-15 percent, although any other fires were too small and/or short-lived to be noted via satellite or advised to the NWS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A late afternoon 30- to 40-acre grass fire burned through 400 PM EST when a fire crew was on site.  More fire crews arrived soon after and the fire was extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow and intense lake effect snow band developed during the late afternoon and evening hours of October 17th. Wet, heavy snow accumulations of 1 to 3 inches led to downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lake effect snow band clipped portions of Allen County with 1 to 3 inches of heavy wet snow. This combined with leaves on tress led to several reports of downed trees and power outages across the county. Roads also became snow covered for a time with a few accidents reported across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a period of moderate to heavy snow during the afternoon and evening hours of February 17th. Total snow accumulations ranged between 2 and 7 inches, with some light ice and sleet accumulations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a brief period of sleet and freezing rain, and then over to all snow during the afternoon hours of February 17th. The snow, moderate to heavy at times, continued into the evening hours. Heavy snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour reduced visibilities to less than a quarter of a mile at times which led to difficult travel conditions. Snowfall totals across the county generally ranged between 5 and 7 inches, with 5.9 inches reported 3 miles north-northeast of Fish Lake. Light ice and sleet accumulations were also achieved before the switch over to all snow occurred. There were reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around a deep 500 mb low centered just north of the Great Lakes, a shortwave pivoted east-southeast and intensified as it moved across the the Ohio Valley region. Temperatures fell well below freezing ahead of it, due to a cold front that swept through the previous day. Temperatures in the upper teens to lower 20's resulted in a dry, fluffy snow. These cold temperatures also allowed snow to quickly accumulate on roads.  Snowfall totals averaged an inch or two across southwest Indiana.

A well-defined upper level shortwave trough shifted northeast across Missouri into the Ohio Valley, where it flattened out within a broader, strong southwesterly flow regime. The resultant mid level forcing was not very strong across the warm sector of the surface low. The weak surface low tracked northeast from Arkansas to West Virginia. A period of widespread rain during the late night and early morning hours generated local urban street flooding.

From the morning hours of Wednesday February 2nd through the morning hours on Friday February 4th, a strong winter storm moved through central Indiana while arctic high pressure simultaneously built into the region from northwest to southeast.  The advancement of cold air changed what was initially appreciable rain, to all snow,  with a prolonged period of freezing rain to sleet during this slow transition across many of the region's southern counties.||Snow began across the upper Wabash Valley on the morning of February 2nd with the heaviest snow for most of central Indiana's northern counties occurring during the 2nd's daytime.  The transitional zone then advanced to a line from roughly Terre Haute to Muncie by late day on the 2nd, before significantly slowing its progress to the southeast.  The night of February 2nd saw a lull in accumulating precipitation for several hours over most of the region.||Heavy snow then impacted the Interstate 70 corridor and points south on February 3rd from pre-dawn through the overnight hours, although mixed precipitation types continued for central Indiana's far southern and far southeastern counties through the February 3rd daytime.  Additional light accumulating snow fell through the morning hours of February 4th south of Interstate 70.  The 48-hour period included Indiana State Police responding to 109 crashes, 110 slide-offs, and 284 motorist assists across the Indianapolis district (Boone, Hamilton, Hancock, Hendricks, Johnson, Marion and Shelby Counties).  Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with at least 22 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total Snowfall and sleet ranged from 3.9 to 5.7 inches with the greatest reported total in Elnora.  A Travel Watch was issued for the county due to the impacts of snow and ice on road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms moved across the region on the 17th. Rainfall of 2 to 4 inches created pockets of road flooding and then widespread river flooding in subsequent days. The heavy rain occurred ahead of a cold front as it entered southeast Missouri and southern Illinois from the northwest. Weak waves of low pressure moved northeast along the front. A strong 850 mb jet up to 60 to 70 knots was accompanied by a strong surge of warm moist air through the Mississippi River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some roads were flooded around the area, mostly secondary roads.

The snow changed to freezing drizzle during the evening hours before changing back briefly to snow before ending overnight. The combination of light snow and ice accumulations created hazardous travel conditions across portions of northern Indiana from the late afternoon hours of February 24th through the early morning hours of February 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow moved across the area during the late afternoon and early evening hours of February 24th. Snow accumulations of 1 to 2 inches were reported during the late afternoon and early evening of February 24th before precipitation changed over to freezing drizzle. The combination of light snow accumulations and ice accretions of less than one tenth of an inch produced hazardous travel conditions with reports of slide-offs and accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms moved across the region on the 17th. Rainfall of 2 to 4 inches created pockets of road flooding and then widespread river flooding in subsequent days. The heavy rain occurred ahead of a cold front as it entered southeast Missouri and southern Illinois from the northwest. Weak waves of low pressure moved northeast along the front. A strong 850 mb jet up to 60 to 70 knots was accompanied by a strong surge of warm moist air through the Mississippi River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Patoka River rose above flood stage and remained there into March. The river crested in March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the morning hours of Wednesday February 2nd through the morning hours on Friday February 4th, a strong winter storm moved through central Indiana while arctic high pressure simultaneously built into the region from northwest to southeast.  The advancement of cold air changed what was initially appreciable rain, to all snow,  with a prolonged period of freezing rain to sleet during this slow transition across many of the region's southern counties.||Snow began across the upper Wabash Valley on the morning of February 2nd with the heaviest snow for most of central Indiana's northern counties occurring during the 2nd's daytime.  The transitional zone then advanced to a line from roughly Terre Haute to Muncie by late day on the 2nd, before significantly slowing its progress to the southeast.  The night of February 2nd saw a lull in accumulating precipitation for several hours over most of the region.||Heavy snow then impacted the Interstate 70 corridor and points south on February 3rd from pre-dawn through the overnight hours, although mixed precipitation types continued for central Indiana's far southern and far southeastern counties through the February 3rd daytime.  Additional light accumulating snow fell through the morning hours of February 4th south of Interstate 70.  The 48-hour period included Indiana State Police responding to 109 crashes, 110 slide-offs, and 284 motorist assists across the Indianapolis district (Boone, Hamilton, Hancock, Hendricks, Johnson, Marion and Shelby Counties).  Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with at least 22 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 7.1 to 9.0 inches with the greatest reported totals near and east of Muncie.  A Travel Watch was issued for the county due to the impacts of snow on road conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Wabash River. At Mount Carmel, the river crested at 26.4 feet on the 28th. Flood stage is 19 feet. Some farm operations were affected, such as moving equipment to higher ground. Extensive lowland flooding occurred, affecting numerous local river roads. The flooding continued into March.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the White River. Considerable flooding of low-lying fields occurred in the flood plain. The flooding affected agricultural and rural residential areas. Several rural county roads extensively flooded. Several small oil fields were inaccessible. State Road 257 south of Washington began to flood. At the Petersburg river gage, the river crested at 23.87 feet on the 26th. Flood stage is 16 feet. The flooding continued into March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front sagged slowly southeast across the region from the 1st to the 4th. Heavy rainfall on the 2nd was followed by snow and sleet on the 3rd and 4th. Rainfall totals and liquid water equivalents during this time totaled between 2 and 3 inches. Some minor river flooding ensued on the Ohio River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Ohio River. Low-lying woods and fields near the river were underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of low pressure moving along a cold front brought rain to central Indiana then a wintry mix as colder air moved in. The rain along with a soggy ground from snowmelt caused both areal and stream/river flooding across central Indiana. The wintry mix caused slick conditions on area roads. Up to 3 inches of snow fell across the Lafayette area with amounts to the south and east generally less than an inch. Total rain amounts were generally between 1.75 inches and 2.50 inches.  Numerous minor accidents occurred across central Indiana due to the slippery conditions along with stalled cars that drove into standing water.  No specific locations were noted with many of these minor incidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts across the county ranged from 1.5 inches to near 3 inches.  2 significant car accidents occurred along I-65 due to the slippery condition with 5 cars damaged. Additional slide offs and minor accidents occurred across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across northwest Indiana on February 17th producing a period of heavy snow during the afternoon and evening.  Winds were gusting to 30 mph producing widespread blowing and drifting snow.  Numerous traffic accidents were reported.  Interstate 65 was closed in multiple locations.  Numerous semi trucks and other vehicles littered the interstate for 20 miles from Lowell to Remington.  The Indiana State Police received 131 calls for help with some motorists spending nearly 17 hours stuck on the interstate.  At least 10 crashes resulted in injuries.  A semi truck crashed near mile marker 193 in Remington shortly after that stretch of interstate was reopened.  The driver of that semi truck died from his injuries.||Some of the highest storm total snowfall amounts included 9.0 inches in Morocco; 8.0 inches in Rensselaer and two miles southeast Crown Point; 7.5 inches in Hobart; 7.3 inches in South Haven; 7.2 inches near Kentland; 6.5 inches in Valparaiso; 6.0 inches in Lowell, DeMotte and Chesterton; 5.8 inches in Porter; 5.6 inches in Dyer; 5.4 inches in St. John and 5.0 inches in Schererville.

An arctic front pushed south across southern Indiana on Thursday, February 3, 2022. Southerly flow and a layer of warm air aloft combined with falling surface temperatures to produce an impactful winter storm with a variety of precipitation types. Significant freezing rain and sleet accumulations caused travel impacts and some power outages. Many areas across southern Indiana received around a quarter inch of ice accumulation, followed by heavy sleet and finally light snow toward the end of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager and trained spotters reported between 0.1 and 0.25 inches of ice accumulation, 0.5 inches of sleet accumulation, and between a trace and 0.5 inches of snow accumulation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports were received from across Clark County from an NWS Employee, members of the public, and broadcast media. Total ice accumulation ranged from 0.1 to 0.25 inches, with 1 to 3 inches of sleet and 0.75 inches of snow reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm dumped significant amounts of snow, sleet, and freezing rain across southwest Indiana. The highest snow amounts were along the White River from Petersburg (Pike County) west across Hazleton (Gibson County), where a few inches of snow fell on top of a layer of sleet about an inch deep. Elsewhere north and west of Evansville, an area of heavy sleet up to an inch was topped by an inch or two of snow. From Evansville eastward to Spencer County, where precipitation was almost all sleet and freezing rain, one-quarter to one-third inch of ice was under about one inch of sleet. Widespread major travel impacts were reported across the region. Roads were covered with an icy mix that resulted in traffic accidents. Scattered power outages occurred in the icing area, primarily from Evansville eastward. A strong cold front moved slowly southeast across the lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri on February 2nd. Precipitation changed from rain to a mix of wintry precipitation on the evening of the 2nd. North and west of Evansville, the cold air became deep enough for some snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread sleet and freezing rain occurred during the evening of the 23rd, followed by some patchy freezing drizzle or light rain overnight. A heavier round of precipitation on the 24th had little if any impact due to temperatures inching just above freezing. Ice and sleet accumulations were generally under one-quarter inch. The wintry precipitation on the evening of the 23rd caused very hazardous travel during the evening commute. A car flipped over on the Lloyd Expressway in Evansville. The wintry precipitation was caused by a moist southwest flow aloft, associated with a 500 mb trough over the western Plains. At the same time, surface high pressure advancing from the northern Plains produced a cold northerly surface wind. The cold low levels enabled the precipitation to stay freezing rain and sleet.

From the morning hours of Wednesday February 2nd through the morning hours on Friday February 4th, a strong winter storm moved through central Indiana while arctic high pressure simultaneously built into the region from northwest to southeast.  The advancement of cold air changed what was initially appreciable rain, to all snow,  with a prolonged period of freezing rain to sleet during this slow transition across many of the region's southern counties.||Snow began across the upper Wabash Valley on the morning of February 2nd with the heaviest snow for most of central Indiana's northern counties occurring during the 2nd's daytime.  The transitional zone then advanced to a line from roughly Terre Haute to Muncie by late day on the 2nd, before significantly slowing its progress to the southeast.  The night of February 2nd saw a lull in accumulating precipitation for several hours over most of the region.||Heavy snow then impacted the Interstate 70 corridor and points south on February 3rd from pre-dawn through the overnight hours, although mixed precipitation types continued for central Indiana's far southern and far southeastern counties through the February 3rd daytime.  Additional light accumulating snow fell through the morning hours of February 4th south of Interstate 70.  The 48-hour period included Indiana State Police responding to 109 crashes, 110 slide-offs, and 284 motorist assists across the Indianapolis district (Boone, Hamilton, Hancock, Hendricks, Johnson, Marion and Shelby Counties).  Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with at least 22 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 6.5 to 8.6 inches with the greatest reported totals near both Pike and Whitestown.  A Travel Warning was issued for the county which is the highest level of local travel advisory due to the extreme impacts of the snow on road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front pushed south across southern Indiana on Thursday, February 3, 2022. Southerly flow and a layer of warm air aloft combined with falling surface temperatures to produce an impactful winter storm with a variety of precipitation types. Significant freezing rain and sleet accumulations caused travel impacts and some power outages. Many areas across southern Indiana received around a quarter inch of ice accumulation, followed by heavy sleet and finally light snow toward the end of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County emergency management officials and trained spotters reported 0.25 inches of ice accumulation, up to 1 inch of sleet, and up to a half inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed over to a prolonged heavy snow event February 2nd into February 3rd. Total snow accumulations ranged between 8 and 16 inches creating significant impacts to travel and commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed over to snow early on February 2nd, with periods of moderate to heavy snow continuing through the late evening hours of February 3rd. There was a report of 17.0 inches of total snow accumulation at Leiters Ford, with 12 to 17 inches common across the county. There were slide-offs and accidents across the area, with school and several businesses closed due to the heavy snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a period of moderate to heavy snow during the afternoon and evening hours of February 17th. Total snow accumulations ranged between 2 and 7 inches, with some light ice and sleet accumulations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a brief period of sleet and freezing rain, and then over to all snow during the afternoon hours of February 17th. The snow, moderate to heavy at times, continued into the evening hours. Heavy snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour reduced visibilities to less than a quarter of a mile at times which led to difficult travel conditions. Snowfall totals across the county generally ranged between 5 and 7 inches, with a report of 6.0 inches 2 miles north-northwest of Osceola. Light ice and sleet accumulations were also achieved before the switch over to all snow occurred. There were reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm dumped significant amounts of snow, sleet, and freezing rain across southwest Indiana. The highest snow amounts were along the White River from Petersburg (Pike County) west across Hazleton (Gibson County), where a few inches of snow fell on top of a layer of sleet about an inch deep. Elsewhere north and west of Evansville, an area of heavy sleet up to an inch was topped by an inch or two of snow. From Evansville eastward to Spencer County, where precipitation was almost all sleet and freezing rain, one-quarter to one-third inch of ice was under about one inch of sleet. Widespread major travel impacts were reported across the region. Roads were covered with an icy mix that resulted in traffic accidents. Scattered power outages occurred in the icing area, primarily from Evansville eastward. A strong cold front moved slowly southeast across the lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri on February 2nd. Precipitation changed from rain to a mix of wintry precipitation on the evening of the 2nd. North and west of Evansville, the cold air became deep enough for some snow.

A winter storm moved across northwest Indiana on February 17th producing a period of heavy snow during the afternoon and evening.  Winds were gusting to 30 mph producing widespread blowing and drifting snow.  Numerous traffic accidents were reported.  Interstate 65 was closed in multiple locations.  Numerous semi trucks and other vehicles littered the interstate for 20 miles from Lowell to Remington.  The Indiana State Police received 131 calls for help with some motorists spending nearly 17 hours stuck on the interstate.  At least 10 crashes resulted in injuries.  A semi truck crashed near mile marker 193 in Remington shortly after that stretch of interstate was reopened.  The driver of that semi truck died from his injuries.||Some of the highest storm total snowfall amounts included 9.0 inches in Morocco; 8.0 inches in Rensselaer and two miles southeast Crown Point; 7.5 inches in Hobart; 7.3 inches in South Haven; 7.2 inches near Kentland; 6.5 inches in Valparaiso; 6.0 inches in Lowell, DeMotte and Chesterton; 5.8 inches in Porter; 5.6 inches in Dyer; 5.4 inches in St. John and 5.0 inches in Schererville.

From the morning hours of Wednesday February 2nd through the morning hours on Friday February 4th, a strong winter storm moved through central Indiana while arctic high pressure simultaneously built into the region from northwest to southeast.  The advancement of cold air changed what was initially appreciable rain, to all snow,  with a prolonged period of freezing rain to sleet during this slow transition across many of the region's southern counties.||Snow began across the upper Wabash Valley on the morning of February 2nd with the heaviest snow for most of central Indiana's northern counties occurring during the 2nd's daytime.  The transitional zone then advanced to a line from roughly Terre Haute to Muncie by late day on the 2nd, before significantly slowing its progress to the southeast.  The night of February 2nd saw a lull in accumulating precipitation for several hours over most of the region.||Heavy snow then impacted the Interstate 70 corridor and points south on February 3rd from pre-dawn through the overnight hours, although mixed precipitation types continued for central Indiana's far southern and far southeastern counties through the February 3rd daytime.  Additional light accumulating snow fell through the morning hours of February 4th south of Interstate 70.  The 48-hour period included Indiana State Police responding to 109 crashes, 110 slide-offs, and 284 motorist assists across the Indianapolis district (Boone, Hamilton, Hancock, Hendricks, Johnson, Marion and Shelby Counties).  Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with at least 22 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 5.0 to 6.2 inches with the greatest reported total near Brazil.  A Travel Watch was issued for the county due to the impacts of snow and ice on road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow area of slow moving, back building thunderstorms caused a swath of locally heavy rain from parts of Whitley county northeast into Steuben county. A small area in the Tri Lakes area of Whitley county received 3 to nearly 5 inches of rain in a few hours, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported nearly 20 homes, as well as several local ditches and roads, were impacted by flash flooding as some locations in the Tri Lakes area received between three and five inches of rain over a few hour period. Some homes experienced a foot or more of water into the interior areas as well as crawlspaces and basements. Washouts of driveways, foundations and some local roads occurred. Damage is estimated at least at $300,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable air moved into portions of east central Indiana as a warm front and weak area of low pressure impacted the region. While several thunderstorms developed and remained below severe levels, a few storms produced precipitation loaded downdrafts and isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a few large trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of heavy rainfall sent the White River above flood stage. The first round was on the morning of the 12th, and the second round of thunderstorms was on the late afternoon and evening of the 13th. The flooding was minor on the White River. Low-lying fields and cropland were flooded. A number of rural county roads were affected, including about ten in Pike County. Low oil fields were underwater.

Two rounds of heavy rainfall sent the White River above flood stage. The first round was on the morning of the 12th, and the second round of thunderstorms was on the late afternoon and evening of the 13th. The flooding was minor on the White River. Low-lying fields and cropland were flooded. A number of rural county roads were affected, including about ten in Pike County. Low oil fields were underwater.
Following a very wet March, a somewhat drier pattern in April allowed flooding to recede on the Wabash River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding ended on the Wabash River very early in the month. Some low-lying cropland was underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough over the Midwest and a moist, buoyant warm sector over the region led to a line of strong storms over southern central Indiana by the early evening.  As a squall line organized and enveloped downstream cells, the resulting bowing segments led to damaging winds and broad rotation in a bookend vortex radar signature.  Straight line wind damage occurred along the path of the bowing segment, with significant damage to boat docks on Lake Monroe in Monroe County, as well as the Country Squire Lakes trailer park in Jennings County.  The region of broad rotation eventually lead to a brief tornado in south-central Decatur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed individual trees on Salt Creek Road and Valley Branch Road and Railroad Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Morning scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms crossed central Indiana amid warm and moist advection atop a relatively dry and stable lower atmosphere.  Weak instability prevented organized stronger storms, although winds at Indianapolis Int'l Airport did briefly reach severe magnitude.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measured at IND Indianapolis Int'l Airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough over the Midwest and a moist, buoyant warm sector over the region led to a line of strong storms over southern central Indiana by the early evening.  As a squall line organized and enveloped downstream cells, the resulting bowing segments led to damaging winds and broad rotation in a bookend vortex radar signature.  Straight line wind damage occurred along the path of the bowing segment, with significant damage to boat docks on Lake Monroe in Monroe County, as well as the Country Squire Lakes trailer park in Jennings County.  The region of broad rotation eventually lead to a brief tornado in south-central Decatur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broad rotation within the squall line's bookend vortex radar signature eventually led to a brief tornado in southeast Decatur County.  The only damage was to a lean-to roof and wall on the east side of a farm outbuilding. This section was demolished with sheet metal roofing and wood debris thrown between 200 and 400 yards to the north and north-northwest.  A small horse trailer was picked up and thrown to the north side of County Road 800 South. There were no scour or drag marks visible in the ground from where the trailer originally sat. A 2x4 was driven into the ground about 20 yards north of the damaged building.  No other damage was found in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Robust breezes downed a large dead tree in Owen County, killing two children.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Robust breezes downed a large dead tree at Indian Oaks Campground in northeastern Owen County, which fell on a golf cart that two children were in, killing both.  Nearby ASOS in Bloomington, IN showed peak gusts that afternoon as high as 41 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lower atmosphere's well-mixed layer reached higher than normal levels, leading to both strong temperature changes with height as well as very dry low-levels by late day.  An area of rain showers moved east across central Indiana during the early evening.  The descending rain evaporated while falling, which cooled and accelerated the descending air. Resultant wind gusts, along the leading edge of the showers, reached 45 mph or greater at the IND, BMG, GEZ and MIE ASOS. An upper jet stream in excess of 100 knots also promoted these enhanced low level winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measured at MIE Muncie Delaware County Airport ASOS.

A broken line of convection associated with the front caused wind damage across four southern Indiana counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure center in Illinois moved to lower Michigan during the evening of the 30th. As a result, a strong cold front crossed the Ohio Valley as a vigorous low level jet was in place. Significant winds with damaging gusts were found across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms occurred. The first round during the mid afternoon hours was associated with a surge of higher dew points northward. Storms erupted along this effective front over southwest Indiana, which was beneath a region of warm moist wind flow at 850 mb. Very large hail accompanied a few of these storms. The second round was an organized complex of thunderstorms that accompanied a pronounced mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) that tracked across Missouri and Illinois. A corridor of moderate to strong instability formed ahead of the MCV from southern Missouri into southern Illinois and Indiana. After a brief lull over Illinois, the storms redeveloped during the evening over the Wabash Valley region as a small cluster with an embedded supercell. The supercell produced a tornado that tracked into southwest Indiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms over parts of southern Illinois intensified as it moved into an  increasingly unstable air mass over Indiana as temperatures warmed into the 80's. Gradually strengthening southwesterly winds in the low and mid levels of the atmosphere aided in the organization of a line of storms. The line of storms posed a gradually increasing threat for damaging winds as it encountered a more unstable air mass. Small bowing clusters of storms produced several damaging wind gusts over southwest Indiana.  The storms formed in the vicinity of a cold front as it sagged southeast toward western Indiana, southern Illinois, and south central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small tree limbs were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado crossed from Wabash County, Illinois into Gibson County. The tornado affected only a small corner of Gibson County between East Mt. Carmel and the White River. The tornado then crossed the White River into Knox County, Indiana, where it paralleled the Gibson County line near the White River to U.S. Highway 41 and beyond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously moist environment that promoted adequate instability, combined with a low level jet stream from the southwest to produce two lines of thunderstorms across central Indiana from late evening on March 5th through dawn on March 6th.  The second line strengthened along and north of Interstate 70 where it produced embedded strong to severe storms.  Damage was most severe in southwestern Madison County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening storm system tracking to the northwest of the region produced strong low-level southerly flow over central Indiana, including a low level jet (LLJ) of 60-70 mph within the 850mb and 925mb levels, from the afternoon of the 30th through the early morning of the 31st.  Isolated to briefly scattered severe magnitude wind gusts occurred from late evening to early morning over central Indiana with nearly all damage from downed trees and poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Indianapolis Metro Airport Awos.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first tornadic damage was observed along U.S. Route 421 near Jan-Cen-Del Community Schools and extended northeast and then east before dissipating just north of State Road 350, just west of Finks Road. There were several areas that sustained straight line wind damage of similar wind magnitude outside of this path.||The first notable damage was broken tree branches and chimney damage along U.S. Highway 421 just west of the school. The tornado caused roof damage at the school and damaged other light objects around the school. A light pole in the athletic fields north of the school was also knocked down. Blowing debris also caused damage to some buses parked at the school.||Further east, the tornado traveled just north of State Route 350, where additional trees were either uprooted or snapped. Snapped trees resulted in substantial damage to an outbuilding in the 700 block of West State Route 350.||While additional damage of similar strength was observed along West Fairground Road and other locations to the north and east of Osgood, this damage was attributed to wind damage from the line of strong storms that swept through the area at the same time as the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated late day thunderstorms across southern central Indiana included a slow-moving cell in northeastern Jackson County that spawned a weak and very brief landspout tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weak tornado touched down and within a minute lifted in nearly the same location. A picnic table was blown to the west and a nearby barn had its roof lifted and blown|to the east and south. Some of these metal panels damaged a fence downwind. Nearby grass showed convergence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread winds from the south-southwest, sustained at 15-20 MPH with gusts 35-40 MPH brought record warmth to the area and very low relative humidity.  The combination of these factors lead to a small wildfire to the southwest of Nashville, IN.  Other impacts nearby included reduced traffic flow on Interstate 70 in Morgan County due to minor grass fires along the roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 20-acre forest fire burned through the late day and early evening hours along the south side of Green Valley Road.  Five houses along Kelp Grove Road were evacuated as they were less than 1 mile downwind of the fire.  12 volunteer fire departments from both Brown and adjacent counties assisted in extinguishing the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering frontal boundary slowly advanced northward in the early pre-dawn hours on March 7th.  This brought a near-record moist environment and adequate instability across southern central Indiana, which combined with a low level jet stream from the southwest to produce an eastward advancing line of thunderstorms with locally severe winds. Damage was most severe in Sullivan, Shelby and Jackson Counties where winds likely gusted to 90-100 mph amid small downbursts.  Insufficient evidence was present to suggest any tornadoes occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds that may have been a very localized microburst at Indian Creek High School moved a van east of its original location and rolled an empty trailer several hundred yards.  No damage to nearby structures or buses in parking lot.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused extensive damage from near Courtland into west side of Seymour with everything blown to the east.  A metal storage barn had sheet metal ripped off and many trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of accumulating snow shifted southeast across southwest Indiana during the late afternoon and early evening hours. Visibility was reduced to about one-half mile in the heaviest part of the band. Most places received one to two inches of snow. Since pavement temperatures were above freezing, most of the accumulation was on grassy areas. However, some slushy accumulations on roadways caused locally hazardous travel.  Storm total snowfall reports included 2 inches near Poseyville (Posey County), 1.5 inches near Rockport (Spencer County) and Princeton (Gibson County), and 1 inch in the Evansville area. The snow was caused by a surface low pressure system which organized over the southern Plains and then tracked along a stationary front that was stretched along the Ohio River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms affected the region. The first round was during the evening, when small clusters of intense storms moved northeastward, producing isolated hail up to the size of nickels. These warm frontal storms were associated with a strengthening low-level jet and some cooling aloft. Late in the night, a line of thunderstorms extended along a cold front from southwest Indiana southwest through far southeast Missouri. As the line moved east, a few bowing segments within this line produced strong winds. Instability was  very limited, but strong winds aloft contributed to the production of isolated strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with a larger low pressure system move east through the Lower Ohio Valley on the evening of the 18th. This caused thunderstorms to move over southern Indiana and north central Kentucky, resulting in multiple reports of severe wind damage and five tornadoes across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was observed touching down by two eyewitnesses near West Wilson Lane and Dog Trot Road at 8:06 PM EDT. Multiple hardwood trees were uprooted and a house suffered significant roof damage on West Wilson Lane. A barn suffered significant roof damage and two other outbuildings had minor damage. On the same property, a carport was lifted and thrown several hundred feet downstream. Debris from this property was also found on a farm approximately half a mile to the east.||The most intense damage occurred near the intersection of Mill Creek Road and Fort Hill Road. A well-constructed brick home had only minor damage to siding, shingles, and porch columns, but multiple trees were uprooted both northwest and northeast of the house. Just down the hill to the northeast, dozens of trees were uprooted or snapped, all laying nearly due north at an angle to the tornado`s northeastward motion. Maximum wind speeds at this point were estimated at 105 mph (EF-1), and the maximum path width at 200 yards.||The tornado continued northeast across open fields and then uprooted or snapped several trees, and caused minor shingle damage to two homes along Woodland Drive in the Naugle Addition subdivision. Two large hardwood trees were uprooted and other large branches were downed as the tornado continued northeast across Orchard Road.||As the tornado moved into the city of Salem a large tree was uprooted and another tree lost a large branch at Crown Hill Cemetery. The tornado continued into the Public Square, where it caused roof and exterior damage to two businesses located to the northwest of the Court House. The tornado lifted at 8:14 PM EDT just to the north of the square.||No fatalities or injuries were reported as a result of the tornado. All the residents our survey team spoke with had received the Tornado Warnings via some combination of Wireless Emergency Alerts, local TV broadcasts, or NOAA Weather Radio, and quickly sought shelter. Most of them took shelter in basements. Of those without a basement, one family took shelter in an interior bathroom, while another resident sought shelter with a neighbor whose house has a basement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken squall line of showers and thunderstorms produced scattered pockets of strong winds and a brief tornado. The strongly forced squall line was accompanied by a few transient meso-vortices within an environment of very strong low level wind shear. Very limited instability reduced the longevity of these meso-vortices. Dew points in the upper 50's ahead of the line proved sufficient for a brief tornado. A very strong low level jet in excess of 80 knots supported the wind damage and tornado events along the line. Lightning was absent at times from the line. The line formed ahead of a strong cold front that trailed southward from a low pressure center passing north of the region. A very strong 500 mb shortwave pivoted east-northeast from the southern Plains, further enhancing the convection despite weak instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of trees were uprooted or snapped. Two houses experienced shingle and gutter damage. Drone footage was used to assist with this survey. The starting point of this tornado was within 100 yards of the Posey County line. Peak winds were estimated near 90 mph.

A line of strong to locally severe thunderstorms tracked across much of northern Indiana producing pockets of damaging winds as well as a brief tornado in Cass County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down south of the intersection of W CR 400 N and N CR 925 W causing damage to some trees as well as a house chimney. The tornado moved quickly northeast and ripped off a enclosed porch from a residence at the intersection of W CR 400 N and N CR 900 W, throwing it around 175 feet over the house. Some trees were damaged on the property with a hanging shed door being blown off (east facing) due to intrusion of wind on the south side from a broken window and open door. Vehicles in the shed were not damaged. An empty silo to the north of the house was thrown along the road with debris into the adjacent field (this was straight line wind related). The debris ended out in the open field on the NE side of the intersection noted above. A pine tree on the SE side of the intersection was also snapped and thrown into this same field from the SW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River flooding continued from February into mid March. During March, the flooding was minor on the White, Wabash, Patoka, and portions of the Ohio Rivers. Low-lying fields and woodlands were inundated, primarily dormant cropland. The flooding was caused by several rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms on the 17th of February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy lake effect snow affected far northern Indiana late afternoon on November 19th through the early morning hours of November 20th. Intense snowfall rates with near zero visibility created difficult travel conditions. Snowfall accumulations up to near 10 inches were reported in the South Bend area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers, temperatures falling into the teens, and gusty winds to 40 mph developed behind an arctic cold front late on November 19th into the early morning hours of November 20th. Reduced visibilities and brief intense snowfall rates were observed with more intense lake effect bands, especially during the evening hours on November 19th. Blowing snow and snow covered roads occurred as a result with difficult travel and a few accidents reported across the region. Snow accumulations were estimated to be in the 1 to 3 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and rather humid air mass across central Indiana, combined with an approaching cold front in the afternoon to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms.  Wind damage was focused over the region's south-central counties;  the line also spawned two brief EF0 tornadoes before a longer track EF1 lifted and touched down several times across southern Shelby County.  A forth and final EF1 tornado tracked briefly over far southwestern Shelby County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fourth tornado to impact central Indiana on May 21st, an EF1, uprooted and snapped trees as the very small tornadic circulation moved through the Auburn Hills subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms with widespread damaging winds affected the Evansville area. The storms initially formed over Illinois and west central Indiana along the residual outflow boundary left by an early morning thunderstorm complex. The atmosphere was very unstable during the late afternoon and early evening, when peak mixed-layer capes approached 4000 J/kg. Once the storms formed an organized cold pool, new thunderstorms developed on the western flank of the storm complex as an unstable southwest wind flow was lifted over the cold pool.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread damaging winds from 60 to 70 mph swept across the Evansville metropolitan area from north to south. A few businesses suffered roof  damage. Hundreds of trees were snapped, uprooted, or had large branches broken. Some of the trees landed across power lines. Some downed trees blocked roads. Numerous power outages occurred. Up to 28,000 electric customers in and near Evansville were without power in the aftermath of the storm. The first report of a downed tree and power lines was about ten miles north of downtown Evansville, or three miles northeast of Darmstadt. Tree damage became more widespread as the storms moved across the north side of the Evansville metro area and then across downtown Evansville. A peak wind gust of 68 mph was measured by the Evansville airport automated observing system. The duration of wind gusts above 60 mph lasted for at least four minutes at the airport. There was an unofficial measured gust of 62 mph a couple miles south of Darmstadt.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread damaging winds continued their path through the metropolitan area. Areas  east and southeast of downtown Evansville received considerable tree damage. The most intense wind damage was in and around Wesselman Park, which is located just east of downtown Evansville. Winds estimated near 75 mph downed numerous trees in that area. The roof of an athletic building at the park was damaged. A trained spotter estimated winds gusted to 65 mph just southeast of downtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on August 20th, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across southern Indiana. While the vast majority of thunderstorms were sub-severe, one cluster of cells managed to grow strong enough to produce wind damage and hail across portions of Floyd and Clark County. Instability was fairly uniform across the area, with generally between 1500-2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE available. However, in the vicinity of the severe cells, a maximum in both mid-level lapse rates and effective bulk shear existed, which may have helped to enhance the severe potential of the near-storm environment. Ultimately, several observations of quarter-size hail and wind damage occurred across Floyd and Clark County before the storm weakened below severe levels.

During the afternoon and evening hours of August 6th, showers and thunderstorms developed across southern Indiana in the presence of sufficient instability. Ample moisture was present across the region, with surface dewpoints in the low-to-mid 70s and precipitable water values approaching 2 inches. The scattered clusters of showers and thunderstorms which developed moved very slowly thanks to weak steering flow and cell propagation was largely driven by cold pool motion and colliding outflow boundaries. One particularly heavy cell was able to sustain itself long enough to produce flash flooding over portions of Floyd County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Road 64 was shut down due to water flowing over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy, wet lake effect snow developed during the late morning hours of November 16th, and persisted through the early morning hours of November 17th. Snowfall totals generally ranged between 4 and 8 inches, heaviest near the Indiana Toll Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy lake effect snow accumulated to between 3 and 8 inches, heaviest near the Indiana Toll Road. There were reports of 5.4 in South Bend and 7.2 1 mile east-northeast of Granger. Visibilities were reduced to a quarter of a mile or less in heavier snow. The heavy, wet nature to the snow led to some downed tree limbs and brief power outages. Roads become slick and snow covered, with reports of accidents and slide-offs in the area. School delays and closings were also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow showers, temperatures falling into the teens, and gusty winds to 40 mph developed behind an arctic cold front late on November 19th into the early morning hours of November 20th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours on August 9th, a nearly stationary surface front was located over southern Indiana. Along and south of this boundary, temperatures surged into the mid-to-upper 80s with dewpoints reaching the low-to-mid 70s, contributing to the development of around 2000 J/kg MLCAPE. This instability and moisture promoted the development of scattered convection across the region during the afternoon hours, most of which was sub-severe. However, in the early evening hours, a particularly strong cluster of storms developed near the Ohio River as incoming storms from south central Indiana caught up to ongoing convection over northern Kentucky. The collision of these two areas of thunderstorms produced a briefly-severe thunderstorm which produced wind damage in Jefferson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees blew down, and some siding came off of a building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized flooding occurred in association with a thunderstorm complex over southeast Illinois and the lower Wabash Valley. Numerous showers and thunderstorms occurred on the leading edge of a southwest wind flow of warm and moist air. High pressure over the central Gulf coast states combined with low pressure over the upper Missouri Valley resulted in the southwest wind flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half a foot of water was over the road near Springfield Road and Williams Road, six miles southeast of New Harmony. Half a foot of water was over Highway 66 and Goodman Road, five miles east of New Harmony. Seven miles northeast of Mt. Vernon, a water rescue was conducted on Caborn Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary area of showers and thunderstorms occurred ahead of a cold front extending from Lake Michigan down across central Missouri. A small pocket of flash flooding occurred under this stationary cluster of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of water flowed through a campground in Santa Claus. Roads were flooded in the northern part of the county, including County Road 1600 North near Santa Claus and Monte Casino Road north of St. Meinrad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample instability and low-level vertical wind shear across central Indiana combined with a warm frontal boundary approaching from the south to produce several rotating, supercell thunderstorms.  These storms produced large hail within a two hour late day period over several of the region's southwestern to south-central counties||Soon after, a rotating cluster of thunderstorms crossed southern central Indiana during the evening and early morning hours.  This relative small, yet intense, complex of storms produced a long-track EF-1 tornado from Illinois into Gibson and Knox Counties in Indiana, as well as isolated, locally intense, damaging winds that were mainly in southeastern central Indiana.

These storms produced large hail within a two hour late day period over several of the region's southwestern to south-central counties||Soon after, a rotating cluster of thunderstorms crossed southern central Indiana during the evening and early morning hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm produced estimated 1.25 inch hail that was found along road after partially melting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low, slowly approaching the region over Missouri, combined with clearing afternoon skies to provide adequate instability, while east-southeasterly surface winds promoted ample low-level wind shear.  Amid this environment, several strong, northward-moving, rotating thunderstorms formed over west-central portions of central Indiana through early evening.  One cell spawned a brief EFU tornado in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm spawned a brief tornado approximately one mile to the north-northeast of the Interstate 74 and Indiana State Route 25 interchange.  Tornado was reportedly seen on the ground, but no damage was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A humid air mass situated over central Indiana, up to a warm front stretched across the region's far-northern counties, provided ample instability;  while adequate levels of vertical wind shear were also present, especially closer to the boundary.  Within this environment, numerous late day, strong thunderstorms moved quickly through the region from south to north.  Early in the evening, scattered wind damage was produced by the two most prominent cells, in Madison County and across several of the region's north-central counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped a large tree in half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line raced through the area and resulted in areas of significant wind damage. Several trees were downed or snapped, power outages were reported, and a high school football scoreboard was bent to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized west-to-east line of early morning thunderstorms tracked from east-central Illinois into central Indiana.  Strong upper level winds and ample vertical wind shear combined with adequate instability to produce severe winds generally parallel to, and north of Interstate 70.  Strongest winds were found where the wind shear was best oriented to the squall line's progression:  across the Indianapolis metro area and points east.  A gustnado in Anderson (Madison County) also caused minor damage.  The episode's strongest winds likely occurred in the evening when a more isolated thunderstorm impacted Rush County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees downed by thunderstorm wind gusts on Camp Street near the  elementary school. Powerlines were also affected by the tree damage.

Eyewitnesses reported the landspout lifted and then dropped in open fields for a few minutes, with no further damage noted, while tracking to the east-northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A humid air mass situated over central Indiana, up to a warm front stretched across the region's far-northern counties, provided ample instability;  while adequate levels of vertical wind shear were also present, especially closer to the boundary.  Within this environment, numerous late day, strong thunderstorms moved quickly through the region from south to north.  Early in the evening, scattered wind damage was produced by the two most prominent cells, in Madison County and across several of the region's north-central counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds removed the roof from a large metal garage and deposited it a short distance away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild and rather moist lower levels combined with an approaching, weakening cold front to generate numerous early afternoon thunderstorms across central Indiana.  Given the weak flow and low-shear environment, most storms were single-cellular with a very slow southerly storm motion.  Amid scattered heavy rainfall rates were a couple reports of marginally large hail between Indianapolis and Bloomington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter sized hail covering roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A humid air mass situated over central Indiana, up to a warm front stretched across the region's far-northern counties, provided ample instability;  while adequate levels of vertical wind shear were also present, especially closer to the boundary.  Within this environment, numerous late day, strong thunderstorms moved quickly through the region from south to north.  Early in the evening, scattered wind damage was produced by the two most prominent cells, in Madison County and across several of the region's north-central counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a 6 inch healthy tree limb onto the roof of a Jeep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample instability and low-level vertical wind shear across central Indiana combined with a warm frontal boundary approaching from the south to produce several rotating, supercell thunderstorms.  These storms produced large hail within a two hour late day period over several of the region's southwestern to south-central counties||Soon after, a rotating cluster of thunderstorms crossed southern central Indiana during the evening and early morning hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado crossed a county line 10 times in its 26.7-mile track from Illinois to Indiana, see below for lat/long/location/time details for each border crossing.||The tornado first entered the extreme southwestern corner of Knox County at 940 PM EST and proceeded to cause damage consistent with EF0/EF1 intensity:  snapping two hardwood trees that fell onto power lines on South 1300 West at 13.4 miles west, breaking large hardwood tree branches on South County Road 1300 West at 18.3 miles south, and leaning two utility poles on South 1200 West at 19.9 miles south.||The tornado's center track then crossed the Knox-Gibson county line 4 times over a 1.4-mile segment roughly midway between Mt. Carmel, IL and Hazleton, IN before re-entering Knox County to cause damage consistent with EF0 intensity during 944-949 PM EST:  breaking small hardwood tree limbs on South County Road 775 West at 18.6 miles south;  and then along the 17.8 South parallel, blowing over 5 irrigation sections at 8.7 miles west, before blowing over two more irrigation sections and blowing in a barn door and tree damage at 8.1 miles west;  and then breaking large hardwood tree branches along South County Road 775 West at both 17.0 miles south and 16.55 miles south.||The tornado track then entered the appendage of Gibson County west of Hazleton, IN for 0.5 miles before again re-entering Knox County to cause damage consistent with EF1 intensity at 952 PM EST:  leaning utility poles and snapping several tree branches on West County Road 1750 South just west of Old US Route 41.||The tornado track then entered Gibson County for the final time while transecting the appendage north of Hazleton, IN for 1.0 miles before re-entering Knox County for the final time to cause damage consistent with EF0/EF1 intensity at 954 PM EST in the town of Decker, IN:  uprooting large hardwood trees along Broadway Street near 3rd Street, with large hardwood branches snapped across southern and eastern portions of Decker.||The tornado continued to the northeast, next causing damage consistent with EF0/EF1 intensity during 955-959 PM EST:  snapping branches of various sizes from both softwood and hardwood trees along Indiana Route 241 and East County Road 1250 South to the west of Iona, IN;  as well as destroying an old barn on East 1250 South just east of South Decker Road by lifting the roof which allowed the walls to collapse.||The tornado's final observed damage was consistent with EF1 intensity during 1000-1003 PM EST :  snapping several softwood trees on South Hart Road just northwest of Upper River Deshee, and fully destroying a barn on Southeast County Road 200 East east of Upper River Deshee, scattering debris several hundred feet to the northeast.||Further data regarding every county/state border crossing.|ILLINOIS:  traveled 8.33 miles as EF0-EF2 before entering Gibson County Indiana.|INDIANA: traveled 18.36 miles as EF0-EF1: Gibson Co. (3.51 mi), Knox Co. (14.85 mi).|A, 38.410/-87.745, Mt. Carmel 1.1 ESE, 2139EST, Illinois to Indiana;     |B, 38.417/-87.732, Mt. Carmel 1.8 E, 2140EST, Gibson to Knox;     |C, 38.446/-87.674, Mt. Carmel 5.3 ENE, 2143EST, Knox to Gibson;     |D, 38.448/-87.669, Mt. Carmel 5.6 ENE, 2143EST, Gibson to Knox;     |E, 38.450/-87.664, Mt. Carmel 5.9 ENE, 2143EST, Knox to Gibson;     |F, 38.455/-87.651, Mt. Carmel 6.7 ENE, 2144EST, Gibson to Knox;     |G, 38.490/-87.586, Hazleton 2.3 W, 2150EST, Knox to Gibson;     |H, 38.494/-87.577, Hazleton 1.9 W, 2150EST, Gibson to Knox;     |I, 38.507/-87.546, Hazleton 1.2 N, 2152EST, Knox to Gibson;     |J, 38.515/-87.530, Decker 0.4 SSW, 2153EST, Gibson to Knox;|K, 38.609/-87.400, Monroe City 2.5 WSW, 2203EST, End of track in Knox Co, IN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and rather humid air mass across central Indiana, combined with an approaching cold front in the afternoon to produce a line of strong to severe thunderstorms.  Wind damage was focused over the region's south-central counties;  the line also spawned two brief EF0 tornadoes before a longer track EF1 lifted and touched down several times across southern Shelby County.  A forth and final EF1 tornado tracked briefly over far southwestern Shelby County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The third tornado to impact central Indiana on May 21st, a long-track EF1, skipped along its entire path.  Many trees were downed or uprooted, with the damage path maximized in town of Mount Auburn where tornado width reached 100 yards.  Peak winds estimated at 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough and associated cold front slowly approached central Indiana, focusing the transport of deep moisture into the region.  Several rounds of strong thunderstorms impacted much of the area from late afternoon on the 29th into the early morning of the 30th.  Mean tropospheric wind as low as 15 knots promoted slow storm motion, which led to locally heavy 1-day rainfall totals and isolated flash flooding from near Terre Haute to the Indianapolis Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall led to water flowing over several roads near Castleton.  Standing water was deeper than 12 inches on parts of 86th street.  The area's greatest rainfall report was 2.90 inches at the Castleton 2 S COOP site, with radar estimates of 2.00-2.50 inches for the area where flooding occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding on Gallagher Road, Baldwin Street and Robertson Road.  Following 1-day rainfall of 0.50-1.00 through the morning on the 29th;  the area's greatest 1-day rainfall report through the morning of the 30th included 3.38 inches in north-central Vigo County with radar-estimates of 4.00-5.00 inches over the area where flooding occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized west-to-east line of early morning thunderstorms tracked from east-central Illinois into central Indiana.  Strong upper level winds and ample vertical wind shear combined with adequate instability to produce severe winds generally parallel to, and north of Interstate 70.  Strongest winds were found where the wind shear was best oriented to the squall line's progression:  across the Indianapolis metro area and points east.  A gustnado in Anderson (Madison County) also caused minor damage.  The episode's strongest winds likely occurred in the evening when a more isolated thunderstorm impacted Rush County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed by thunderstorm wind gusts at the intersection of Raymond Street and Sherman drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rather large and slow-moving area of thunderstorms moved east across the lower Ohio Valley. The storms produced heavy rainfall and isolated urban flooding. The storms occurred ahead of a cold front that extended from central Indiana southwest to the Ozark Mountains of Arkansas. A very moist and unstable air mass supported the storms. Southwest 850 mb winds increased to over 30 knots, further aiding storm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 2.5 inches of rain and flooding of a driveway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of early evening thunderstorms on the 24th caused isolated pockets of tree damage across central Indiana with sub-severe hail also across Hendricks county.  By late into the evening, a series of storms moved across Knox county with areas of rainfall over 6 inches.  Significant flash flooding occurred across the county with a road washed out and water rescues conducted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm wind gusts around Greencastle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a cluster of strong storms over southern Lake Michigan early morning that organized further as they tracked into northwest Indiana/southwest Michigan. The most intense line of storms peeled east-southeastward and hammered Elkhart/Kosciusko/Whitley counties with widespread wind damage. Additional storms also caused wind damage elsewhere across northern Indiana. Some severe hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning and early afternoon hours on July 6th, scattered showers and thunderstorms along a stationary front across central Indiana intensified and moved southward. As these storms moved into southern Indiana, they strengthened further thanks to a highly unstable environment, with mixed-layer CAPE approaching 3000 J/kg. The storms, which were initially somewhat discrete, grew upscale as they approached the Ohio River, with cold pool mergers helping to organize new convection. Sufficient downdraft CAPE helped damaging winds reach the surface, with scattered wind damage reports being observed across southern Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were trees and power lines down along Doolittle Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another period of hazardous heat occurred across southwest Indiana. Heat indices peaked around 105 degrees on the 22nd, 23rd, and 24th. The peak heat index at Evansville was 105 on the 22nd, 105 on the 23rd, and 107 on the 24th. Another large 500 mb high became stubbornly persistent across the southern states, from the Four Corners region to the Southeast. Underneath this high, daily high temperatures were in the mid 90's. Overnight lows were mostly in the mid 70's.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon hours on July 24th, strong to severe thunderstorms developed across southeastern Illinois and southwestern Indiana ahead of a surface cold front located immediately to the northwest. Ahead of the cold front, temperatures across southwestern Indiana surged into the low-to-mid 90s during the afternoon on the 24th, with dewpoints residing in the mid-70s. This allowed for 3000-4000 J/kg of mixed-layer CAPE, leading to explosive convective development during the mid-to-late afternoon hours. By the time storms entered south central Indiana, decreased insolation reduced instability somewhat; however, storms were still strong enough to produce isolated wind damage across Dubois and Crawford County. As the cold front's forward progress had nearly stalled, the moist unstable air mass persisted over southern Indiana into the early overnight hours, leading to continued elevated convection which contributed to isolated flash flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported flooding along US 231 at the intersection with railroad tracks in Huntingburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two girls, a 9 year old and a 14 year old, were spotted struggling in Lake Michigan in a portion of Marquette Beach that is unguarded during the afternoon of July 12th.  Beachgoers were able to rescue the 14 year old but the 9 year old slipped under the water and was unable to be located.  First responders found the 9 year old who was transported to a hospital and was later pronounced dead.  Northerly winds were blowing all day with waves peaking between 3 and 4 feet at the Michigan City buoy during the late morning of July 12th.  Waves were 2 to 3 feet at the time of the drowning.

A 72 year old man went under water in Lake Michigan late in the morning of July 10th at the Indiana Dunes State Park.  He did not resurface.  Lifeguards found the man 15 minutes after he went under, submerged in 7 feet of water about 100 feet from shore.  He was taken to a hospital in critical condition and later died.  Waves at the time at the nearby Michigan City buoy were 1 to 2 feet.  However, north winds had been blowing all night with waves peaking between 4 and 5 feet during the evening of July 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours of July 8th, thunderstorms remained nearly stationary across portions of central Indiana with a period of extremely heavy rain across much of Greene county and portions of Sullivan and Vigo counties. A quasi-tropical pattern led to increased rainfall rates with reports as great as 6.19 at the Graysville 5 WNW COOP site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains flooded numerous roads in the town of Sullivan and across Sullivan County.  Water then receded shortly after 1100 am EST.  No specific damage was reported from flooding.

The rain fell over the course of several hours with amounts of over 6 inches in spots. Conditions had been fairly dry in the days leading up which limited the extent of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple road closures across much of the county with most severe flooding in Muncie.  Several cars stalled in the floodwaters with rescues needed. 24 rainfall amounts were highest in Muncie and Yorktown with reports of 6.00 and 6.26 inches at each location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave gripped southwest Indiana for a few days. From the 4th to the 6th, peak afternoon heat indices ranged from 101 to 112 degrees. The hottest day in southwest Indiana was the 5th, when the heat index peaked at 109 degrees at the Evansville airport.   During this heat wave, a sprawling 500 mb high covered most of the southern states from the southern Plains to the southern Appalachian Mountains. Beneath this large 500 mb high, daily high temperatures soared to near 100 degrees. Night-time temperatures were quite warm. Evansville set a daily record warm low temperature of 80 degrees on the 6th.  The temperature hit 101 degrees in Evansville on July 5, marking the first time since 2012 the city broke the century mark.

Four separate rounds of thunderstorms impacted central and northern portions of Rush County through the afternoon and early evening of July 5th, with tree damage noted during the second round.  A couple of trees that were downed or damaged near Carthage and the northside of Rushville had been weakened by severe storms on July 1st.  Later in the evening, an isolated storm caused widespread minor tree damage to Muncie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12-15 inch tree downed by thunderstorm wind gusts on the north side of Rushville. According to the emergency manager the tree was likely weakened by the storms on July 1st, and estimated winds of 50-55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly-approaching cold front combined with ample instability from temperatures as high as the mid-90s to produce isolated thunderstorms across central Indiana through late day hours on the 1st.  Damaging winds were confined to a downburst in Rushville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm downburst winds snapped or uprooted trees from Rush County Fairgrounds across northern and northeastern portions of Rushville.  Falling trees damaged 4 vehicles and 2 homes.  Winds downed tents, trailers awnings and tables at the Rush County Fair, leading to flying debris that possibly caused injuries.  The top half of a communication tower was bent over and onto the Rushville Police Department.  Another tree was downed at 815 East 11th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered intense thunderstorms fired up during the heat of the day across southwest Indiana. This activity developed ahead of a weak cold front and surface low centered over the mid Mississippi Valley. The atmosphere was quite unstable, with mixed-layer capes from 1500 to 2500 J/kg. Wind shear was sufficiently strong for an isolated storm to become severe, with large hail. Effective bulk shear was around 30 to 35 knots.

Additional storms also caused wind damage elsewhere across northern Indiana. Some severe hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning and early afternoon hours on July 6th, scattered showers and thunderstorms along a stationary front across central Indiana intensified and moved southward. As these storms moved into southern Indiana, they strengthened further thanks to a highly unstable environment, with mixed-layer CAPE approaching 3000 J/kg. The storms, which were initially somewhat discrete, grew upscale as they approached the Ohio River, with cold pool mergers helping to organize new convection. Sufficient downdraft CAPE helped damaging winds reach the surface, with scattered wind damage reports being observed across southern Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple trees that were uprooted and power lines blown down in Scottsburg.
