EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure crossing the region on the 20th promoted northwest winds gusting as high as 30 MPH.  Despite unseasonably cold temperatures ranging from the teens to the low 30s, very low dewpoints near 0 degrees Fahrenheit led to minimum relative humidity values on the 20th around 20 percent.  The combination of these factors lead to a large brush fire in Brown County.||Other small fires likely occurred across central Indiana amid the very low relative humidity values, although they were too small and/or short-lived to be noted via satellite or advised to the NWS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 110-acre brush fire burned, within rough terrain on the east side of Brown County State Park and away from any structures, from the evening of the 20th through at least late on the 22nd when fire crews completed operations.  Three volunteer fire crews worked with the Indiana Department of Natural Resources and conservation officers to create a looping 2.97-mile fire line around the fire's perimeter by sundown on the 21st.  Through the 25th, marginal fire weather conditions continued, with daily minimum relative humidity values around 25-30 percent, and daily wind gusts continuing to peak around 30 MPH.  The Brown County EMA reported isolated smoldering hot spots likely continued through at least December 5th, 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, occluded mid-latitude storm system tracking north-northeastward from the Quad Cities area, featured a strengthening upper-level wave.  The wave's negatively-tilting trough axis created a strong wind field about 1 mile above the surface at the 850 millibar pressure level.  This caused generally 50-60 mph wind gusts at the surface across central Indiana during mainly morning and midday hours on the 5th.  The strongest, isolated wind gusts reached 58-65 mph, mainly across Tippecanoe County and Clinton County.  Isolated damage reports were led by several counties east of Indianapolis that each reported multiple downed trees and/or numerous power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site at the interchange between I-65 and Indiana Route 28, 1.3 miles east of Fickle, recorded a peak gradient wind gust of 56 kts.  The Frankfort AWOS, FKR (4.9 miles to the east) recorded a peak gust of 50 kts at 835 EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds developed across northwest Indiana during the morning of November 5th and continued into the afternoon.  Peak wind gusts included 64 mph near Rensselaer and near New Chicago, 61 mph in East Chicago and 58 mph in Valparaiso.  Wind gusts were estimated to 60 mph in Portage where several trees were blown down in yards along Eleanor Street.  A small tree was uprooted near South Haven.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 61 mph was measured at the Cline Avenue Bridge in East Chicago.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours of September 3rd, a combination of a moist, unstable environment and relatively light steering level winds over the Ohio Valley facilitated the development of slow moving showers and thunderstorms which produced heavy rainfall across southern Indiana. Several broken lines of convection drifted from west to east across the region, spurred on by 1000-1500 J/kg MLCAPE and Precipitable Water values approaching 2 inches. A weak mid-level low across the mid-Mississippi Valley also contributed to broad uplift across the region, with precipitation being widespread across most of the lower Midwest and Ohio Valley during the afternoon of September 3rd. Where heavy thunderstorms lingered the longest, flash flooding was reported, with rainfall reports exceeding 10 inches in isolated areas. This heavy rainfall contributed to particularly severe flash flooding in Jefferson County, Indiana along Brushy Fork, where several homes were washed away and one fatality was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 64 year old woman was killed when water left the banks of Brushy Fork and swept away her house on Brushy Fork Road. A barn and all the family's belongings were also swept away in the flood waters. The body of the woman was found 5 miles downstream by family members after first responders suspended their efforts overnight. The value of the damaged property is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of locally slow-moving thunderstorms resulted in isolated instances of flash flooding. An elongated mid-level trough and associated closed low stretched through the middle Mississippi and lower Ohio Valleys, helping to focus a stationary front across the region. Sufficient daytime heating helped generate moderate instability in the vicinity of the front. Where the slow-moving storms were locally focused near the front, isolated excessive rainfall occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water completely covered the intersection of Highways 57 and 68.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed Indiana from west to east during the early afternoon to early evening of the 22nd, as an area of low pressure deepened along this passing boundary. The storm system intensified rapidly over the following 36 hours while tracking through the Great Lakes and into Quebec. The gradient between this storm and very strong arctic high pressure over the central United States brought strong westerly winds, gusting locally as high as 56 mph at Muncie.||The combination of these winds with near-record low temperatures behind the frontal passage made for dangerously low wind chills across the region, as low as -46 degrees in Hamilton County, from late on the 22nd through the morning of the 24th. Wet roads at the time of the frontal passage quickly led to surfaces flash freezing through the evening of the 22nd, resulting in hazardous travel conditions. Other, less-intense impacts included generally 1-2 of snowfall through the night of the 22nd, and subsequent blowing snow and drifting snow that reduced visibility as low as 1/4 to 3/4 mile.||An indirect weather-related fatality from an automotive accident occurred in Arlington (Rush Co.) early on the 24th.  A 19-hour period from mid-afternoon on the 22nd to late morning on the 23rd included Indiana State Police responding to 54 crashes (9 with injuries), 13 slide-offs, and 66 motorist assists across the Indianapolis district (Boone, Hamilton, Hancock, Hendricks, Johnson, Marion and Shelby Counties).  This 7-county sub-region also saw 3 troopers struck by motor vehicles, including 1 injury, while trying to assist other incidents.  Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with 7 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category.  Portions of US Highway 421 to the northwest of Indianapolis, as well as numerous secondary roads across central Indiana, were closed for several days due to weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures early on the 23rd were as low as -10F, while strong winds gusted as high as 46 mph...resulting in extreme wind chills as low as -35F.  Snowfall as great as 2.0 inches combined with the strong winds to produce blowing snow and drifting snow, with visibility as low as 1/2 mile...while a Travel Watch was issued for the county due to the impacts of extreme cold and blowing snow on road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very early season snowfall blanketed southwest Indiana during the early morning hours. Accumulations ranged from two to three inches. Most of the snow fell in just 1 to 3 hours. Visibility was below one mile in the heaviest snow bands, and even down to one-quarter mile at the Evansville airport. Because temperatures had been in the 60's, 70's, and even lower 80's in the days preceding the snow, pavement temperatures were such that the snow melted on roads. However, snowfall rates were high enough to briefly cover bridges and overpasses, resulting in a few accidents. This unseasonably early snowfall was the seventh earliest snow event of an inch or more at Evansville, where 2.5 inches fell. The earliest was on the day before Halloween of 1993 (3.7 inches). The sixth earliest was on November 11, 2019, when 1.1 inches fell. The snow occurred in cold northerly flow behind a strong cold front and ahead of a deep 500 mb trough from the upper Missississippi Valley to the Ozark plateau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary surface front and meandering mid-level cyclone continued to promote unsettled weather across the Ohio Valley on September 5th. During the morning hours, a slow-moving cluster of showers and thunderstorms across southwestern Indiana fed off of increasing diurnally-driven instability and a relatively moisture-rich environment, resulting in heavy rainfall across portions of Dubois County. In the community of Dubois, some minor flooding was observed, with rainfall totals of between 3 and 4 inches being reported in the vicinity of flood reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A school basement in the town of Dubois flooded, and local emergency services were requested to help remove water from the school. Rainfall totals of between 3 and 4 inches were observed in the vicinity of the flooding reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward-advancing cold front combined with unseasonably hot and humid conditions and marginal vertical wind shear late on the 21st to produce a broken line of strong thunderstorms across southern central Indiana.  Isolated downbursts produced wind damage in Rushville and portions of Decatur and Jennings Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm downburst winds blew down trees and power lines in central Decatur County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward-advancing cold front combined with unseasonably hot and humid conditions and marginal vertical wind shear late on the 21st to produce a broken line of strong thunderstorms across southern central Indiana.  Isolated downbursts produced wind damage in Rushville and portions of Decatur and Jennings Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm downburst winds caused damage along Base Road from County Road 575 North to County Road 850 North, and to the northeast from that area.  Several large trees were uprooted while winds downed power lines, broke off power poles, and bent flagpoles to the ground.  Residences were impacted, as well as a plant farm where winds leveled a greenhouse and ripped plastic off of 2 others.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers intensified on the evening of November 12th and continued through the early morning hours of November 13th.  The heaviest snow accumulations occurred across portions of north central and northeast St. Joseph County where 4 to 8 inches of snowfall were received. Impacts from the snowfall increased during the early morning hours on November 13th as temperatures dropped below freezing allowing for snow-covered roads to develop and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell during the evening hours of November 12th extending through much of the early morning hours of November 13th.  Total snowfall ranged from 4 to 8 inches.  An official NWS observation of 7.5 inches of snow was reported in South Bend, IN at 0700 November 13th. The snow was heavy and wet in nature and started to accumulate on roadways overnight as temperatures dropped below freezing.  Roads became slick with some slide-offs reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to briefly heavy snow fell during the evening hours of November 12th extending through much of the early morning hours of November 13th.  Total snowfall ranged from 2 to 5 inches.  The snow was heavy and wet in nature and started to accumulate on roadways overnight as temperatures dropped below freezing.  Roads became slick with some slide-offs reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed Indiana from west to east during the early afternoon to early evening of the 22nd, as an area of low pressure deepened along this passing boundary. The storm system intensified rapidly over the following 36 hours while tracking through the Great Lakes and into Quebec. The gradient between this storm and very strong arctic high pressure over the central United States brought strong westerly winds, gusting locally as high as 56 mph at Muncie.||The combination of these winds with near-record low temperatures behind the frontal passage made for dangerously low wind chills across the region, as low as -46 degrees in Hamilton County, from late on the 22nd through the morning of the 24th. Wet roads at the time of the frontal passage quickly led to surfaces flash freezing through the evening of the 22nd, resulting in hazardous travel conditions. Other, less-intense impacts included generally 1-2 of snowfall through the night of the 22nd, and subsequent blowing snow and drifting snow that reduced visibility as low as 1/4 to 3/4 mile.||An indirect weather-related fatality from an automotive accident occurred in Arlington (Rush Co.) early on the 24th.  A 19-hour period from mid-afternoon on the 22nd to late morning on the 23rd included Indiana State Police responding to 54 crashes (9 with injuries), 13 slide-offs, and 66 motorist assists across the Indianapolis district (Boone, Hamilton, Hancock, Hendricks, Johnson, Marion and Shelby Counties).  This 7-county sub-region also saw 3 troopers struck by motor vehicles, including 1 injury, while trying to assist other incidents.  Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with 7 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category.  Portions of US Highway 421 to the northwest of Indianapolis, as well as numerous secondary roads across central Indiana, were closed for several days due to weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures early on the 23rd were as low as -9F, while strong winds gusted as high as 43 mph...resulting in extreme wind chills as low as -37F.  Snowfall as great as 3.5 inches combined with the strong winds to produce blowing snow and drifting snow, with visibility as low as 1/2 mile...while a Travel Warning was issued for the county which is the highest level of local travel advisory due to the extreme impacts of the snow and ice on road conditions.

A decaying line of thunderstorms exited Wisconsin and provided a glancing blow to Michigan and Indiana. There was least one instance of tree damage in northern Indiana and one instance of sub severe hail in Michigan. In the vicinity of this line of storms, wind was strong with gusts near 50mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on Elm Road near Elm Road Elementary.

Periods of lake effect snow, blowing snow, reduced visibilities and gusty winds developed late November 17th and continued through the day on November 18th bringing difficult travel conditions. Snow accumulation of 2 to 7 inches were common across the area, heaviest along the Indiana Toll Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another round of lake effect snow accumulated to between 3 and 7 inches across northern St. Joseph County, heaviest in the South Bend area when snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were reported. South Bend officially received 6.5 inches of snow accumulation during this time. Temperatures falling into the 20s, snow already on the ground, and gusty winds also allowed for areas of blowing snow and reduced visibilities. Travel was difficult across the area with reports of slide-offs, accidents and school closings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward-advancing cold front combined with unseasonably hot and humid conditions and marginal vertical wind shear late on the 21st to produce a broken line of strong thunderstorms across southern central Indiana.  Isolated downbursts produced wind damage in Rushville and portions of Decatur and Jennings Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm downburst winds blew down trees through the town of Rushville and along the south side of town...damaging 6 homes, including partial roof damage to a building on the southwest side of Rushville.  Winds continued to down trees and powerlines to the southeast side of Rushville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours in a warm and humid airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several creeks were out of their banks in the vicinity of Aberdeen Road, mainly to the west of Aberdeen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very early season snowfall blanketed southwest Indiana during the early morning hours. Accumulations ranged from two to three inches. Most of the snow fell in just 1 to 3 hours. Visibility was below one mile in the heaviest snow bands, and even down to one-quarter mile at the Evansville airport. Because temperatures had been in the 60's, 70's, and even lower 80's in the days preceding the snow, pavement temperatures were such that the snow melted on roads. However, snowfall rates were high enough to briefly cover bridges and overpasses, resulting in a few accidents. This unseasonably early snowfall was the seventh earliest snow event of an inch or more at Evansville, where 2.5 inches fell. The earliest was on the day before Halloween of 1993 (3.7 inches). The sixth earliest was on November 11, 2019, when 1.1 inches fell. The snow occurred in cold northerly flow behind a strong cold front and ahead of a deep 500 mb trough from the upper Missississippi Valley to the Ozark plateau.

Slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours in a warm and humid airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to 10 inches of rain over a few hour period caused significant flash flooding over portions of western Switzerland County. Numerous creeks and streams were out of their banks. The hardest hit areas were in the vicinity of Bennington and along Indian Creek, where several county bridges were heavily damaged and a couple of bridges were washed out. Significant damage occurred to numerous homes along Indian Creek with trees also downed by the flowing water and roadways along the creek washed out in places.

The storm system intensified rapidly over the following 36 hours while tracking through the Great Lakes and into Quebec. The gradient between this storm and very strong arctic high pressure over the central United States brought strong westerly winds, gusting locally as high as 56 mph at Muncie.||The combination of these winds with near-record low temperatures behind the frontal passage made for dangerously low wind chills across the region, as low as -46 degrees in Hamilton County, from late on the 22nd through the morning of the 24th. This 7-county sub-region also saw 3 troopers struck by motor vehicles, including 1 injury, while trying to assist other incidents.  Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with 7 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of thunderstorms containing rainfall rates up to two inches per hour trained over parts of southwest Indiana. The storms produced some urban flash flooding. The storms intensified during the heat of the day ahead of a 500 mb shortwave trough drifting eastward toward the Ohio River Valley. A tropical air mass was firmly entrenched, with precipitable water values well above the 90th percentile for early September. Effective vertical wind shear from 20 to 25 knots resulted in loosely organized bands of storms. The mean steering flow was parallel to the synoptic forcing, which caused training of the bands of storms with heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread street flooding occurred in the Evansville metro area. The water was waist deep in spots. Some water rescues were conducted from stranded vehicles. In the large retail area on the east side of Evansville, Green River Road was flooded both north and south of the Lloyd Expressway. Flash flooding was reported all along Highway 41 in Evansville. The storm total rainfall was about three inches at rain gages in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed Indiana from west to east during the early afternoon to early evening of the 22nd, as an area of low pressure deepened along this passing boundary. The storm system intensified rapidly over the following 36 hours while tracking through the Great Lakes and into Quebec. The gradient between this storm and very strong arctic high pressure over the central United States brought strong westerly winds, gusting locally as high as 56 mph at Muncie.||The combination of these winds with near-record low temperatures behind the frontal passage made for dangerously low wind chills across the region, as low as -46 degrees in Hamilton County, from late on the 22nd through the morning of the 24th. Wet roads at the time of the frontal passage quickly led to surfaces flash freezing through the evening of the 22nd, resulting in hazardous travel conditions. Other, less-intense impacts included generally 1-2 of snowfall through the night of the 22nd, and subsequent blowing snow and drifting snow that reduced visibility as low as 1/4 to 3/4 mile.||An indirect weather-related fatality from an automotive accident occurred in Arlington (Rush Co.) early on the 24th.  A 19-hour period from mid-afternoon on the 22nd to late morning on the 23rd included Indiana State Police responding to 54 crashes (9 with injuries), 13 slide-offs, and 66 motorist assists across the Indianapolis district (Boone, Hamilton, Hancock, Hendricks, Johnson, Marion and Shelby Counties).  This 7-county sub-region also saw 3 troopers struck by motor vehicles, including 1 injury, while trying to assist other incidents.  Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with 7 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category.  Portions of US Highway 421 to the northwest of Indianapolis, as well as numerous secondary roads across central Indiana, were closed for several days due to weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures early on the 23rd were as low as -10F, while strong winds gusted as high as 46 mph...resulting in extreme wind chills as low as -41F.  Snowfall as great as 2.5 inches combined with the strong winds to produce blowing snow and drifting snow, with visibility as low as 1/2 mile...while a Travel Warning was issued for the county which is the highest level of local travel advisory due to the extreme impacts of the snow and blowing snow on road conditions.||An automotive accident early on the 24th on the 7300 block of US Highway 52, on the west side of Arlington resulted in 1 fatality.  Weather was a contributing factor to the accident:  for an unknown reason the driver lost control of the vehicle and due to road conditions was unable to regain control and hit another vehicle head on.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded front along the eastern periphery of a strong, broad upper-level cut-off trough centered over the north-central United States, crossed the region after midnight on the 15th.  Potent winds within a strong low-level jet stream were mixed down to the surface following the frontal passage.  Wind gusts were generally in the 40 to 55 mph range, but one observation at the KLAF ASOS exceeded 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph gradient wind gust was measured by the Purdue University Airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog blanketed southwest Indiana during the night-time and early morning hours. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less at times. High pressure centered over southern Illinois produced conditions favorable for dense fog.

An arctic front associated with a winter storm brought dangerous wind chills to area from the early morning hours of December 23rd to the early morning hours of December 24th. The combination of temperatures in the single digits above zero to single digits below zero and wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph resulted in dangerous wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic front brought sub-zero temperatures and strong wind gusts to 40 to 50 mph. This combination resulted in dangerous wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero from the early morning hours on December 23rd to the early morning hours on December 24th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive damage was reported along State Road 10 and surrounding areas from Demotte to Wheatfield.  Healthy trees were snapped at their base and at least 7 free standing utility poles were snapped in an open field.  A metal roof was torn off a farm building and spread across a field.  Numerous tree limbs were blown down in Wheatfield and shingles were blown off roofs.  A 35 to 40 foot tall tree was uprooted near Route 10 and Route 49.  A spotter measured a wind gust to 78 mph before their equipment failed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across northwest Indiana during the afternoon of August 29th.  Tree damage was reported across many areas of Lake and Newton Counties.  Additional thunderstorms developed in the late afternoon and continued into the late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree limbs were blown down in Newton County with the most concentrated damage near Roselawn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front associated with a winter storm brought dangerous wind chills to area from the early morning hours of December 23rd to the early morning hours of December 24th. The combination of temperatures in the single digits above zero to single digits below zero and wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph resulted in dangerous wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic front brought sub-zero temperatures and strong wind gusts to 40 to 50 mph. This combination resulted in dangerous wind chills of 25 to 35 below zero from the early morning hours on December 23rd to the early morning hours on December 24th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo shared on social media showed a large oak tree that was blown down onto a shed near County Roads W 600 N and N 100 W.  The tree appeared to be partially rotten.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front swept through the region, with wind chills dropping to 20 to 30 below zero. These were the coldest wind chills observed in over 25 years across the region. Evansville plummeted to -30, which was the coldest since December 22, 1989. This was also only the 8th time dating back to 1950 that Evansville has recorded a wind chill dropping down to -30 or colder. The most extreme wind chills were from the evening of the 22nd to the morning of the 23rd, but wind chills as late as the morning of December 24th were often below minus 10. Actual air temperatures only reached the single digits above zero on the 23rd, which followed morning lows around 5 below. Peak wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were observed during this event as well. The peak wind gust at Evansville was 41 mph on the evening of the 22nd. Isolated power outages were reported, some of which were caused by the strong winds. The extreme cold was indirectly responsible for a number of structure fires caused by supplemental heating sources, such as space heaters. There were numerous reports of frozen water pipes that burst and flooded homes and other buildings. An apartment building in Evansville was flooded after a pipe burst. Parts of Princeton in Gibson County were under a boil water advisory due to a water main break. On top of the extreme cold, one to three inches of snow fell. The highest amounts were east of a line from Princeton to Evansville, where around 3 inches fell. Snowfall amounts from Evansville west and north were from 1 to 2 inches. This snow and the accompanying flash freeze heavily impacted travel across the region. Despite relatively light traffic, there were numerous traffic mishaps. A fatal wreck occurred on Interstate 69 on the northeastern outskirts of Evansville. A 64-year-old man was killed, and several others were injured in the two-vehicle accident. One of the vehicles crossed the median and struck another vehicle head-on. Another wreck on Interstate 69 at the Pike/Daviess County line injured one person and prompted the closure of both northbound lanes.

A surface low pressure center and its parent 500 mb shortwave moved south-southeast from the northern Plains across the middle Mississippi Valley. An area of snow in advance of these features produced an inch or two of accumulation during the late night and early morning hours. The snow readily accumulated on road surfaces due to the antecedent frigid temperatures the previous few days. Driving conditions were treacherous during the morning commute, but relatively light traffic on the observed Christmas holiday kept traffic mishaps less numerous than they otherwise might have been. Several vehicles slid off roadways. There was a crash on Interstate 64 eastbound. A multi-vehicle crash on U.S. Highway 41 at Interstate 69 near Evansville injured two people.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front associated with a winter storm brought dangerous wind chills to area from the early morning hours of December 23rd to the early morning hours of December 24th. The combination of temperatures in the single digits above zero to single digits below zero and wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph resulted in dangerous wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic front brought sub-zero temperatures and strong wind gusts to 40 to 50 mph. This combination resulted in dangerous wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero from the early morning hours on December 23rd to the early morning hours on December 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of June 17th, a very warm and humid air mass was present across southern Indiana as a cold front extended west to east across central Illinois into central Indiana. South of the cold front, a Mesoscale Convective System moved across southern Indiana during the mid-morning hours, with storms feeding off the large amounts of instability. Across southern Indiana, the main observed severe hazard was damaging winds, with wind damage being observed across several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment of over 4000 J/KG of SBCAPE, combined with 45 to 60 knots of effective shear set the stage for multiple rounds of thunderstorm during the afternoon and evening hours of June 13th. Initial storms remained poorly organized, but did produce sporadic wind damage or hail. More substantial thunderstorm development evolved during the evening hours in northwestern Indiana and southwestern Lower Michigan and progressed east, eventually producing several swaths of wind damage. The most intense cluster of storms evolved in Berrien county Michigan and dropped rapidly southeast impacting locations extending from South Bend to Fort Wayne. Southwestern portions of Fort Wayne suffered the greatest impacts with widespread tree and structure damage from 80 to nearly 100 mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a large tree was blown down across the road.

A hot and humid southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front brought temperatures up to the lower 90's. Dew points were exceptionally high, with readings in the lower 80's at most stations.

Wind speeds were estimated approaching 70 mph by the time the storm was departing Elkhart county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment of over 4000 J/KG of SBCAPE, combined with 45 to 60 knots of effective shear set the stage for multiple rounds of thunderstorm during the afternoon and evening hours of June 13th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The swath of damaging winds existed southeastern Whitley county and begin to intensify further, impacting the Arcola area with numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines down, some onto structures. The damage continued southeast impacting the southwest portions of Fort Wayne and surrounding areas. The hardest hit areas extended from Aboite township into Indian Village and Waynedale. Tree and tree limb damage was extensive in some areas, causing difficulties in clearing of roadways and restoration of power as numerous power lines and poles were impacted either by the winds alone or falling trees and tree limbs. The Fox Island County Park area had to be closed for an extended period due to extensive tree damage on roads and paths. A 98 mph wind gust was recorded at the Fort Wayne Regional Airport. An 89 year old female was inside her home in the 5400 block of Mason Drive when a tree crashed into her home. She was severely injured by broken glass and passed away at a local hospital despite surgery to stop the bleeding early on the morning of June 14. The fatality was compounded by first responders being unable to reach her for an extended period of time due to the extensive tree and power line debris.

An amazing cold front barged through southern Indiana during the evening of December 22nd. The front caused temperatures to crash 50 degrees in 12 hours, from the 40s on the afternoon of the 22nd to subzero readings the following morning. Light rain ahead of the front quickly changed to light snow behind it with 1 to 3 inches falling in many locations. A band of 4 to 5 inches of snow developed from Salem, Indiana to Frankfort, Kentucky. The snow and plunging temperatures were accompanied by wind gusts to 40 mph and wind chills down to 30 degrees below zero, some of the coldest wind chills seen in 25 years. Daytime readings on the 23rd did not get out of the single digits. Near blizzard conditions were observed during the snowfall with visibility limited to a half mile. People were urged to avoid travel on one of the busiest holiday travel days of the year. Numerous crashes and closures were reported on state highways due to the snow and extreme cold temperatures. The extreme cold also resulted in scattered power outages and rolling blackouts in parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 4 inches accumulated with blowing snow and strong wind gusts of 30-40 mph. Rapidly falling temperatures resulted in slick roads and significant travel impacts. |Treacherous travel conditions were noted throughout December 23rd, a busy holiday travel day. Wind chills of -25 to -30 were observed on the morning of the 23rd.

Southwestern portions of Fort Wayne suffered the greatest impacts with widespread tree and structure damage from 80 to nearly 100 mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey of damage indicated the swath of wind damage continued southeast from Elkhart county, first impacting Syracuse then progressing SSE into North Webster and points to the southeast towards the Noble county line. Tree damage was not widespread in Syracuse, but several tree limbs and trees were noted down across the city. The winds began to accelerate further as they moved off Lake Wawasee and just east of the NWS Northern Indiana office. One of the apartment complexes at Baypoint condos suffered roof damage as the winds caught a corner of the roof that was not properly anchored, causing loss of several pieces plywood and impacting 2 apartments with mainly water damage. The noted tree damage increased on the south side of North Webster and Webster Lake. While overall wind speeds of 60 to 70 mph were estimated, pockets of locally higher gusts likely occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment of over 4000 J/KG of SBCAPE, combined with 45 to 60 knots of effective shear set the stage for multiple rounds of thunderstorm during the afternoon and evening hours of June 13th. Initial storms remained poorly organized, but did produce sporadic wind damage or hail. More substantial thunderstorm development evolved during the evening hours in northwestern Indiana and southwestern Lower Michigan and progressed east, eventually producing several swaths of wind damage. The most intense cluster of storms evolved in Berrien county Michigan and dropped rapidly southeast impacting locations extending from South Bend to Fort Wayne. Southwestern portions of Fort Wayne suffered the greatest impacts with widespread tree and structure damage from 80 to nearly 100 mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey of damage from emergency management officials indicated the swath of damage from Kosciusko county persisted southeast, clipping the southwest portion of Noble county. The damage extended from south of Cromwell towards Knapp Lake, Smalley Lake and Big Lake and towards Merriam. Numerous trees were down along this path with some structure damage noted from falling trees. Outside of the falling tree related damage, some barns and homes were impacted by the actual winds with a small unattached garage, older barn and also a horse barn collapsing. Winds were estimated from 65 to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amazing cold front barged through southern Indiana during the evening of December 22nd. The front caused temperatures to crash 50 degrees in 12 hours, from the 40s on the afternoon of the 22nd to subzero readings the following morning. Light rain ahead of the front quickly changed to light snow behind it with 1 to 3 inches falling in many locations. A band of 4 to 5 inches of snow developed from Salem, Indiana to Frankfort, Kentucky. The snow and plunging temperatures were accompanied by wind gusts to 40 mph and wind chills down to 30 degrees below zero, some of the coldest wind chills seen in 25 years. Daytime readings on the 23rd did not get out of the single digits. Near blizzard conditions were observed during the snowfall with visibility limited to a half mile. People were urged to avoid travel on one of the busiest holiday travel days of the year. Numerous crashes and closures were reported on state highways due to the snow and extreme cold temperatures. The extreme cold also resulted in scattered power outages and rolling blackouts in parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 3 inches accumulated with blowing snow and strong wind gusts of 30-40 mph. Rapidly falling temperatures resulted in slick roads and significant travel impacts. Treacherous travel conditions were noted throughout December 23rd, a busy holiday travel day. Wind chills of -25 to -30 were observed on the morning of the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low crossing Indiana from west to east brought a warm front northward through the region.  The resulting increase in instability, coupled with adequate wind shear both through the 0-6 km layer as well as near the surface, generated scattered thunderstorms over southern and eastern portions of the region.  A few storms produced isolated large hail/damaging winds in the afternoon, before two weak tornadoes were spawned later in the day over east-central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed large tree limb across eastbound County Road 800 South, west of Indiana Route 67.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed Indiana from west to east during the early afternoon to early evening of the 22nd, as an area of low pressure deepened along this passing boundary. The storm system intensified rapidly over the following 36 hours while tracking through the Great Lakes and into Quebec. The gradient between this storm and very strong arctic high pressure over the central United States brought strong westerly winds, gusting locally as high as 56 mph at Muncie.||The combination of these winds with near-record low temperatures behind the frontal passage made for dangerously low wind chills across the region, as low as -46 degrees in Hamilton County, from late on the 22nd through the morning of the 24th. Wet roads at the time of the frontal passage quickly led to surfaces flash freezing through the evening of the 22nd, resulting in hazardous travel conditions. Other, less-intense impacts included generally 1-2 of snowfall through the night of the 22nd, and subsequent blowing snow and drifting snow that reduced visibility as low as 1/4 to 3/4 mile.||An indirect weather-related fatality from an automotive accident occurred in Arlington (Rush Co.) early on the 24th.  A 19-hour period from mid-afternoon on the 22nd to late morning on the 23rd included Indiana State Police responding to 54 crashes (9 with injuries), 13 slide-offs, and 66 motorist assists across the Indianapolis district (Boone, Hamilton, Hancock, Hendricks, Johnson, Marion and Shelby Counties).  This 7-county sub-region also saw 3 troopers struck by motor vehicles, including 1 injury, while trying to assist other incidents.  Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with 7 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category.  Portions of US Highway 421 to the northwest of Indianapolis, as well as numerous secondary roads across central Indiana, were closed for several days due to weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures early on the 23rd were as low as -10F, while strong winds gusted as high as 51 mph...resulting in extreme wind chills as low as -41F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions developed across northwest Indiana during the afternoon of June 13th and continued through the afternoon of June 15th.  The highest heat indices were reached during the afternoon of June 14th and included 109 degrees at Gary Airport and at the Indiana Dunes; 106 degrees at Valparaiso Airport and 105 degrees at Rensselaer Airport.

A strong upper level ridge of high pressure developed across the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys, producing a record-breaking early season heat wave. Evansville broke two daily record highs and three daily record warm lows. Evansville's low of 81 degrees on the 14th tied its all-time warmest low temperature in the month of June. Heat index values exceeded 105 degrees starting on June 13 and continued through June 16. The most oppressive day was June 13th, when dewpoints in the mid 70's to around 80 produced heat index values of 110 to 115.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along a plunging cold frontal boundary, a nocturnal complex of thunderstorms tracked to the east-southeast from Illinois into far southwestern central Indiana.  Copious vertical wind shear and instability, including strong mid-level temperature lapse rates, fueled embedded cells with scattered severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees on both South County Road 800 West and County Road 625 South immediately west of 800 West.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low crossing Indiana from west to east brought a warm front northward through the region.  The resulting increase in instability, coupled with adequate wind shear both through the 0-6 km layer as well as near the surface, generated scattered thunderstorms over southern and eastern portions of the region.  A few storms produced isolated large hail/damaging winds in the afternoon, before two weak tornadoes were spawned later in the day over east-central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Survey confirmed an EF0 skipping tornado tracked 0.57 miles through the town of Summitville.  The initial touchdown was west of the waste water treatment plant in a grove of trees.  At the plant, a poorly secured roof was partially lifted and pieces strewn downwind in a convergent pattern.  A 2x4 was planted into the ground from the southeast which was opposite of the general damage direction.  The tornado then skipped through town causing tree and roof damage before causing more significant damage along Mill Street.  There, several large tree limbs damaged a car and home with insulation splattered across 3 of the 4 sides of the home.  Small amounts of mud splatter was also noted through town.  Damage then briefly lessened near the school before 2 large and unhealthy oak trees were snapped. The tornado lifted afterwards with no additional damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and extremely humid surface conditions provided very high instability, while a slowly- advancing warm frontal zone promoted ample vertical wind shear, which led to widespread afternoon thunderstorms along and north of the I-74 corridor.  Storms produced isolated flash flooding as well as marginally-severe winds and hail, over both north-central and east-central portions of central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm heavy rain led to water flowing over roadways near the 6000 block of Indiana Route 26 and near the intersection of Indiana Routes 26 and 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of June 17th, a very warm and humid air mass was present across southern Indiana as a cold front extended west to east across central Illinois into central Indiana. South of the cold front, a Mesoscale Convective System moved across southern Indiana during the mid-morning hours, with storms feeding off the large amounts of instability. Across southern Indiana, the main observed severe hazard was damaging winds, with wind damage being observed across several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were trees down on US 150.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small clusters of thunderstorms moved southeastward across southwest Indiana. The storms formed along a cold front, which trailed southwest from a surface low centered over eastern Ontario. There was enough diurnal destabilization ahead of the front for isolated to scattered storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and a utility pole were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across northwest Indiana during the late afternoon and early evening of December 22nd.  Temperatures fell from the lower to middle 30s into the single digits within hours.  Snow quickly spread across the region with roads becoming snow covered.  The snow tapered off to snow showers in the evening and snow showers continued through the afternoon of December 23rd.  Total snowfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range with an area of 3 to 5 inches near Lake Michigan.  Winds gusted as high as 55 mph through the morning of December 23rd and continued gusting to 40 mph through that evening producing widespread blowing snow and near whiteout conditions in many areas, especially in rural and open areas.  Blizzard conditions were widespread across Porter County during much of December 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and extremely humid surface conditions provided very high instability, while a slowly- advancing warm frontal zone promoted ample vertical wind shear, which led to widespread afternoon thunderstorms along and north of the I-74 corridor.  Storms produced isolated flash flooding as well as marginally-severe winds and hail, over both north-central and east-central portions of central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Relayed via Social Media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved through the Ohio Valley on June 22nd, with showers and thunderstorms developing just ahead of the front during the afternoon hours. Across southern Indiana, a cluster of disorganized showers and thunderstorms intensified and merged into a line as they approached the Ohio River. Ahead of the cold front, temperatures surged into the mid-90s with dewpoints in the low-to-mid 70s, and storms had large amounts of instability to work with. Storms intensified enough to produce severe wind damage, mainly across Clark and Floyd County before crossing the Ohio River into central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a downed power line hanging down over a street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a northward-progressing warm frontal zone, a strong, broad, subtropical upper-level ridge built over central Indiana by June 14th.  Associated heat and at times record-high dewpoints (for June) then lasted for several days across the region.  Morning lows were mainly in the mid-70s to low 80s, while afternoon highs in the mid-90s were common; dewpoints climbed into the upper 70s on June 14th, and stayed in the mid-70s through June 16th.  Excessive Heat criteria of 110 degree or higher heat indices was reached during the afternoon and/or late day hours of June 14th, in 9 of central Indiana's 39 zones between far southwestern counties, southeastern portions of the Indianapolis Metro area, and two north-central counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Criteria was reached from maximum heat index values reaching 110 degrees on June 14th over central and eastern portions of Johnson County. Heat index values were from measured temperature and dewpoint at the OKK AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized complex of thunderstorms moved east-southeast across southwest Indiana during the early to mid-morning hours. The leading edge of the storms was accompanied by strong winds, which resulted in isolated pockets of tree damage. The biggest swath of tree damage was across the Evansville metro area, where lots of small tree limbs and several whole trees were blown down. The combination of mid-level dry air and very moist low levels, characterized by surface dew points in the 70's, produced strong downdrafts. Although deep-layer wind shear was generally less than 25 knots, the established cold pool with the storm complex sustained the surface gust front. An upper-level ridge across the Plains and a trough over the Northeast produced a northwest wind flow that steered early-day thunderstorm clusters into the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on a home in Princeton. Small tree limbs were down around the city.

The combination of mid-level dry air and very moist low levels, characterized by surface dew points in the 70's, produced strong downdrafts. Although deep-layer wind shear was generally less than 25 knots, the established cold pool with the storm complex sustained the surface gust front. An upper-level ridge across the Plains and a trough over the Northeast produced a northwest wind flow that steered early-day thunderstorm clusters into the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs up to three inches in diameter were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized complex of thunderstorms moved east-southeast across southwest Indiana during the early to mid-morning hours. The leading edge of the storms was accompanied by strong winds, which resulted in isolated pockets of tree damage. The biggest swath of tree damage was across the Evansville metro area, where lots of small tree limbs and several whole trees were blown down. The combination of mid-level dry air and very moist low levels, characterized by surface dew points in the 70's, produced strong downdrafts. Although deep-layer wind shear was generally less than 25 knots, the established cold pool with the storm complex sustained the surface gust front. An upper-level ridge across the Plains and a trough over the Northeast produced a northwest wind flow that steered early-day thunderstorm clusters into the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of strong damaging winds extended from northwest Vanderburgh County southeast across the Evansville metro area. Several large trees and power lines were blown down in Evansville. A wind gust to 58 mph was measured at the Evansville airport's automated weather station. A trained spotter estimated wind gusts to 65 mph on the east side of Evansville. Small tree limbs were down from Darmstadt southeast across the Evansville metro area. Utility crews worked to restore power, but a few thousand customers were without power through the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across northwest Indiana during the late afternoon and early evening of December 22nd.  Temperatures were in the middle 30s ahead of the front and fell into the single digits within hours after the cold front passed.  Low temperatures by the morning of December 23rd ranged from 5 below to 10 below zero.  High temperatures on December 23rd remained in the low single digits below zero.  Winds gusted as high as 55 mph through the morning of December 23rd and continued gusting to 40 mph through the evening of December 23rd.  The combination of strong winds and sub zero temperatures produced wind chills between 30 below and 42 below zero.  While temperatures slowly warmed to near zero by the morning of December 24th, winds continued gusting to 35 mph.  Wind chills finally warmed above 20 below zero by the late morning of December 24th.

An arctic cold front crossed Indiana from west to east during the early afternoon to early evening of the 22nd, as an area of low pressure deepened along this passing boundary. The storm system intensified rapidly over the following 36 hours while tracking through the Great Lakes and into Quebec. The gradient between this storm and very strong arctic high pressure over the central United States brought strong westerly winds, gusting locally as high as 56 mph at Muncie.||The combination of these winds with near-record low temperatures behind the frontal passage made for dangerously low wind chills across the region, as low as -46 degrees in Hamilton County, from late on the 22nd through the morning of the 24th. Wet roads at the time of the frontal passage quickly led to surfaces flash freezing through the evening of the 22nd, resulting in hazardous travel conditions. Other, less-intense impacts included generally 1-2 of snowfall through the night of the 22nd, and subsequent blowing snow and drifting snow that reduced visibility as low as 1/4 to 3/4 mile.||An indirect weather-related fatality from an automotive accident occurred in Arlington (Rush Co.) early on the 24th.  A 19-hour period from mid-afternoon on the 22nd to late morning on the 23rd included Indiana State Police responding to 54 crashes (9 with injuries), 13 slide-offs, and 66 motorist assists across the Indianapolis district (Boone, Hamilton, Hancock, Hendricks, Johnson, Marion and Shelby Counties).  This 7-county sub-region also saw 3 troopers struck by motor vehicles, including 1 injury, while trying to assist other incidents.  Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with 7 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category.  Portions of US Highway 421 to the northwest of Indianapolis, as well as numerous secondary roads across central Indiana, were closed for several days due to weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures early on the 23rd were as low as -10F, while strong winds gusted as high as 46 mph...resulting in extreme wind chills as low as -35F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of June 17th, a very warm and humid air mass was present across southern Indiana as a cold front extended west to east across central Illinois into central Indiana. South of the cold front, a Mesoscale Convective System moved across southern Indiana during the mid-morning hours, with storms feeding off the large amounts of instability. Across southern Indiana, the main observed severe hazard was damaging winds, with wind damage being observed across several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down blocking the roadway.

Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with 7 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category.  Portions of US Highway 421 to the northwest of Indianapolis, as well as numerous secondary roads across central Indiana, were closed for several days due to weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures early on the 23rd were as low as -14F in Rockville, while strong winds gusted as high as 47 mph...resulting in extreme wind chills as low as -45F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved through the Ohio Valley on June 22nd, with showers and thunderstorms developing just ahead of the front during the afternoon hours. Across southern Indiana, a cluster of disorganized showers and thunderstorms intensified and merged into a line as they approached the Ohio River. Ahead of the cold front, temperatures surged into the mid-90s with dewpoints in the low-to-mid 70s, and storms had large amounts of instability to work with. Storms intensified enough to produce severe wind damage, mainly across Clark and Floyd County before crossing the Ohio River into central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on top of a car on 111 South.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a northward-progressing warm frontal zone, a strong, broad, subtropical upper-level ridge built over central Indiana by June 14th.  Associated heat and at times record-high dewpoints (for June) then lasted for several days across the region.  Morning lows were mainly in the mid-70s to low 80s, while afternoon highs in the mid-90s were common; dewpoints climbed into the upper 70s on June 14th, and stayed in the mid-70s through June 16th.  Excessive Heat criteria of 110 degree or higher heat indices was reached during the afternoon and/or late day hours of June 14th, in 9 of central Indiana's 39 zones between far southwestern counties, southeastern portions of the Indianapolis Metro area, and two north-central counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Criteria was reached from maximum heat index values exceeding 110 degrees on June 14th over roughly the southwestern half of Vigo County.  Heat index values were from measured temperature and dewpoint at the HUF Terre Haute ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across northwest Indiana during the late afternoon and early evening of December 22nd.  Temperatures fell from the lower to middle 30s into the single digits within hours.  Snow quickly spread across the region with roads becoming snow covered.  The snow tapered off to snow showers in the evening and snow showers continued through the afternoon of December 23rd.  Total snowfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range with an area of 3 to 5 inches near Lake Michigan.  Winds gusted as high as 55 mph through the morning of December 23rd and continued gusting to 40 mph through that evening producing widespread blowing snow and near whiteout conditions in many areas, especially in rural and open areas.  Blizzard conditions were widespread across Porter County during much of December 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours on June 1st, a cold front moved across southern Indiana. Ahead of the front, warm and humid conditions provided sufficient instability for the development of scattered clusters of strong to severe storms. While most cells were below severe levels, isolated wind damage occurred in a cell which moved across Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles were down along West Washington School Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low crossing Indiana from west to east brought a warm front northward through the region.  The resulting increase in instability, coupled with adequate wind shear both through the 0-6 km layer as well as near the surface, generated scattered thunderstorms over southern and eastern portions of the region.  A few storms produced isolated large hail/damaging winds in the afternoon, before two weak tornadoes were spawned later in the day over east-central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey confirmed EF1 tornado tracked from 4.75 miles east-southeast of Morristown to 3.4 miles north of Rushville.  Tornado first caused damage around the intersection of County Roads West 200 North and North 900 West. Four homes sustained damage that consisted primarily of roof and siding taken off homes. South facing front porch roof taken off western most house. Clear convergent pattern was observed in this area with several large tree limbs down and a few smaller trees topped or uprooted. Sheet metal was thrown nearly a quarter mile east. A storage shed was lifted and moved 40 feet off of its foundation. Siding and roof loss occurred at 3 other homes at this location. The Tornado likely lifted briefly over a large field but then hit the Posey Township Volunteer Fire Department station on US Highway 52 where the south side of the roof was torn off and debris lofted several hundred yards to the northeast. Trees to the west of this building were broken off near their tops. The tornado then continued skipping to the east per law enforcement observations and witnesses. Minor damage was observed at a school on the north side of Arlington with soffit/awning damage on west side of building. That metal was deposited to the north while small trees and limbs along a north side fence line were laid down to the south. From this point on to ending point, witnesses reported the tornado lifting and dropping across fields with only sporadic tree damage. Law enforcement reported the tornado lifted for final time along County Road 300 North, just east of Indiana Route 3 and West of County Road 150 West.

Portions of US Highway 421 to the northwest of Indianapolis, as well as numerous secondary roads across central Indiana, were closed for several days due to weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures early on the 23rd were as low as -10F, while strong winds gusted as high as 49 mph...resulting in extreme wind chills as low as -40F.  Snowfall as great as 2.0 inches combined with the strong winds to produce blowing snow and drifting snow, with visibility as low as 1/4 mile...while a Travel Watch was issued for the county due to the impacts of extreme cold and blowing snow on road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly crossing central Indiana from northwest to southeast combined with adequate instability south of Interstate 70 to produce scattered afternoon thunderstorms.  Several storms became very strong to marginally severe early in the evening, with relatively dry mid-levels promoting isolated large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm produced estimated 1.00 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly crossing central Indiana from northwest to southeast combined with adequate instability south of Interstate 70 to produce scattered afternoon thunderstorms.  Several storms became very strong to marginally severe early in the evening, with relatively dry mid-levels promoting isolated large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm produced a few half-dollar hailstones with much quarter-sized hail northwest of the Interstate 65 and US Highway 31 interchange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West-northwesterly winds along a slowly-advancing warm frontal zone promoted the ample bulk shear and copious instability that produced numerous morning showers and thunderstorms.  Storms tracked eastward along the I-74 corridor, producing isolated large hail, and later isolated wind damage and flash flooding.  A road was washed-out in a possible microburst over northwestern Putnam County.  All severe cells occurred over west-central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm produced 2.00 inch hail.  A photograph from public was received by broadcast media who posted it to Twitter.  Three approximate 2.00-inch hailstones shown in picture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along a plunging cold frontal boundary, a nocturnal complex of thunderstorms tracked to the east-southeast from Illinois into far southwestern central Indiana.  Copious vertical wind shear and instability, including strong mid-level temperature lapse rates, fueled embedded cells with scattered severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees as observed by Johnson Township Fire Department member in Decker, who estimated a 60 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed Indiana from west to east during the early afternoon to early evening of the 22nd, as an area of low pressure deepened along this passing boundary. The storm system intensified rapidly over the following 36 hours while tracking through the Great Lakes and into Quebec. The gradient between this storm and very strong arctic high pressure over the central United States brought strong westerly winds, gusting locally as high as 56 mph at Muncie.||The combination of these winds with near-record low temperatures behind the frontal passage made for dangerously low wind chills across the region, as low as -46 degrees in Hamilton County, from late on the 22nd through the morning of the 24th. Wet roads at the time of the frontal passage quickly led to surfaces flash freezing through the evening of the 22nd, resulting in hazardous travel conditions. Other, less-intense impacts included generally 1-2 of snowfall through the night of the 22nd, and subsequent blowing snow and drifting snow that reduced visibility as low as 1/4 to 3/4 mile.||An indirect weather-related fatality from an automotive accident occurred in Arlington (Rush Co.) early on the 24th.  A 19-hour period from mid-afternoon on the 22nd to late morning on the 23rd included Indiana State Police responding to 54 crashes (9 with injuries), 13 slide-offs, and 66 motorist assists across the Indianapolis district (Boone, Hamilton, Hancock, Hendricks, Johnson, Marion and Shelby Counties).  This 7-county sub-region also saw 3 troopers struck by motor vehicles, including 1 injury, while trying to assist other incidents.  Significant travel impacts were noted throughout the storm, with 7 of 39 central Indiana counties placed under a Travel Warning - the most severe travel category.  Portions of US Highway 421 to the northwest of Indianapolis, as well as numerous secondary roads across central Indiana, were closed for several days due to weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures early on the 23rd were as low as -10F, while strong winds gusted as high as 46 mph...resulting in extreme wind chills as low as -41F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of June 17th, a very warm and humid air mass was present across southern Indiana as a cold front extended west to east across central Illinois into central Indiana. South of the cold front, a Mesoscale Convective System moved across southern Indiana during the mid-morning hours, with storms feeding off the large amounts of instability. Across southern Indiana, the main observed severe hazard was damaging winds, with wind damage being observed across several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was down on US 231.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low crossing Indiana from west to east brought a warm front northward through the region.  The resulting increase in instability, coupled with adequate wind shear both through the 0-6 km layer as well as near the surface, generated scattered thunderstorms over southern and eastern portions of the region.  A few storms produced isolated large hail/damaging winds in the afternoon, before two weak tornadoes were spawned later in the day over east-central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm produced golf-ball sized hail near Hayden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved through the Ohio Valley on June 22nd, with showers and thunderstorms developing just ahead of the front during the afternoon hours. Across southern Indiana, a cluster of disorganized showers and thunderstorms intensified and merged into a line as they approached the Ohio River. Ahead of the cold front, temperatures surged into the mid-90s with dewpoints in the low-to-mid 70s, and storms had large amounts of instability to work with. Storms intensified enough to produce severe wind damage, mainly across Clark and Floyd County before crossing the Ohio River into central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down in Lexington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a northward-progressing warm frontal zone, a strong, broad, subtropical upper-level ridge built over central Indiana by June 14th.  Associated heat and at times record-high dewpoints (for June) then lasted for several days across the region.  Morning lows were mainly in the mid-70s to low 80s, while afternoon highs in the mid-90s were common; dewpoints climbed into the upper 70s on June 14th, and stayed in the mid-70s through June 16th.  Excessive Heat criteria of 110 degree or higher heat indices was reached during the afternoon and/or late day hours of June 14th, in 9 of central Indiana's 39 zones between far southwestern counties, southeastern portions of the Indianapolis Metro area, and two north-central counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Criteria was reached from maximum heat index values exceeding 110 degrees on June 14th over roughly the northern and northeastern half of Johnson County.  Heat index values were from measured temperature and dewpoint at the HFY AWOS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Criteria was reached from maximum heat index values reaching 110 degrees on June 14th over southern portions of Knox County.  Heat index values are estimated from combination of observed maximum temperature of 97 degrees at Washington 1 W station and dewpoints from nearby ASOS/AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 38 year old man entered the water at Porter Beach in northwest Indiana in the late morning of June 27th after seeing a 17 year old girl in distress.  He was able to help the girl get to shore, as they both fought strong currents and waves as high as 5 feet.  The man went under water and did not resurface.  First responders pulled the man from the water shortly after he went under.  He was rushed to a hospital where he died.  The 17 year old girl survived.

A strong upper level ridge of high pressure developed across the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys, producing a record-breaking early season heat wave. Evansville broke two daily record highs and three daily record warm lows. Evansville's low of 81 degrees on the 14th tied its all-time warmest low temperature in the month of June. Heat index values exceeded 105 degrees starting on June 13 and continued through June 16. The most oppressive day was June 13th, when dewpoints in the mid 70's to around 80 produced heat index values of 110 to 115.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures early on the 23rd were as low as -10F, while strong winds gusted as high as 48 mph...resulting in extreme wind chills as low as -39F.  Snowfall as great as 2.0 inches combined with the strong winds to produce blowing snow and drifting snow, with visibility as low as 1/4 mile...while a Travel Watch was issued for the county due to the impacts of extreme cold and blowing snow on road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms associated with an upper level disturbance moved east across the region during the late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed across northern Wayne County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds developed across northwest Indiana during the afternoon of April 14th.  Wind gusts were measured to 64 mph near Kouts; to 62 mph near Remington and to 61 mph at the Valparaiso Airport.
High winds developed across northwest Indiana during the afternoon of April 14th.  Wind gusts were measured to 64 mph near Kouts; to 62 mph near Remington and to 61 mph at the Valparaiso Airport.

A long duration winter storm affected all of northern Indiana with multiple hazards across including heavy snow, blowing snow, strong winds, whiteouts, and dangerous wind chills.  Blizzard conditions were experienced across portions of northwest and north central Indiana.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches across most of northern Indiana, but higher amounts of 5 to 9 inches were reported along and north of Interstate 80 across northwest Indiana due to lake effect and lake enhancement.  The storm brought very difficult to impossible travel conditions across the area.  High impacts to travel were reported even across areas which experienced the lower snowfall totals. Dangerous winds chills of 20 below to 40 below zero were reported from late Friday night through late Saturday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought snow and blowing snow that led to very dangerous travel conditions from late Thursday night through Saturday morning. Most of the storm total snowfall of 1 to 3 inches occurred Thursday night into early Friday, but strong wind gusts of 50 mph and blowing snow led to dangerous travel conditions Friday and Friday night. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported with significantly reduced visibilities at times along with blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amazing cold front barged through southern Indiana during the evening of December 22nd. The front caused temperatures to crash 50 degrees in 12 hours, from the 40s on the afternoon of the 22nd to subzero readings the following morning. Light rain ahead of the front quickly changed to light snow behind it with 1 to 3 inches falling in many locations. A band of 4 to 5 inches of snow developed from Salem, Indiana to Frankfort, Kentucky. The snow and plunging temperatures were accompanied by wind gusts to 40 mph and wind chills down to 30 degrees below zero, some of the coldest wind chills seen in 25 years. Daytime readings on the 23rd did not get out of the single digits. Near blizzard conditions were observed during the snowfall with visibility limited to a half mile. People were urged to avoid travel on one of the busiest holiday travel days of the year. Numerous crashes and closures were reported on state highways due to the snow and extreme cold temperatures. The extreme cold also resulted in scattered power outages and rolling blackouts in parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 2 inches accumulated with blowing snow and strong wind gusts of 30-40 mph. Rapidly falling temperatures resulted in slick roads and significant travel impacts. Treacherous travel conditions were noted throughout December 23rd, a busy holiday travel day. Wind chills of -25 to -30 were observed on the morning of the 23rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought snow and blowing snow that led to blizzard conditions at times from early Friday morning through Saturday afternoon.  Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches along the Toll Road to as high as 10 to 14 inches closer to the Indiana-Michigan stateline.  A cooperative observer reported 14 inches of storm total snowfall 1 mile WSW of LaPorte, IN.  Widespread blowing and drifting snow led to blizzard conditions at times with wind gusts as high as 50 mph. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported which included Interstate 80.  Travel became very difficult to impossible with several road closures reported.
